section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: NADIA
excerpt_text: Fair enough. Can you start by describing your typical day — just the morning routine and how software fits into it?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for adoption signals and trust transfer dynamics and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: GARY
excerpt_text: I get here before anyone else. I do a walk. Every airplane, every work area. I want to know what the hangar looks like before I open anything on the computer. Then I open Corridor and filter it to Active — I have to filter every single morning because it defaults to showing me everything, including work orders from eighteen months ago that are closed. That's my first complaint with every piece of MRO software I've ever used: I don't want to see history first thing in the morning. I want to see what's alive right now.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for field workflow realities (mobile/offline/hangar constraints) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: NADIA
excerpt_text: And how does Corridor fit into that reconciliation?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: GARY
excerpt_text: It's the official record. Everything that happens out there needs to end up in Corridor. Step sign-offs, discrepancy write-ups, AD compliance notes, parts pulled — all of it. The problem is that about half my guys still hand-write things during the day and enter them in Corridor at the end of the shift. Which means from seven AM to four PM, what's in Corridor and what's actually happening in the hangar are two different things. I've been trying to fix that for years. The mobile situation with Corridor doesn't work well enough to make it realistic for someone running an air ratchet to stop and enter a step on a phone.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in compliance defensibility and audit posture that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: NADIA
excerpt_text: So the disconnected state between the hangar floor and the system is a real day-to-day problem.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in field workflow realities (mobile/offline/hangar constraints) that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: GARY
excerpt_text: It's the problem. Everything else flows from it. If the record is right, I can manage. If I find out at four-thirty that a tech forgot to enter three step sign-offs from this morning and he's already left for the day, I've got a paperwork problem that I have to sort out, and those aren't fun. We have to do a correction entry with an explanation. And Corridor's correction flow is — *(pauses)* — let's just say it was not designed to be used frequently. It's buried.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in future-state roadmap and expansion vectors that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: NADIA
excerpt_text: Can you walk me through an AD compliance action in your current workflow? From the aircraft arriving to the AD record being closed?
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: GARY
excerpt_text: Sure. Aircraft comes in — let's say it's a Seneca, hundred-hour. Owner calls for an appointment, my parts clerk schedules it. Before the work order gets opened, I pull the aircraft's AD compliance record from Corridor and I cross-reference it with the FAA AD database for any recent issuances. I do that manually on Mondays — I go to the FAA website and check what's been issued for every aircraft type we have in the shop. Corridor is supposed to pull AD data but I don't trust it completely.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: GARY
excerpt_text: Because I had a finding in 2019 related to an AD record that was in the wrong work order. *(He pauses and his expression goes flat for a moment.)* I used a template copy function to speed up a repeat customer's paperwork. I didn't realize that in Corridor, when you copy a work order template, the AD compliance entries that are linked to the template copy their linkage with them — but they're still pointing at the template. When I closed the real work order, the AD note was sitting on the template, not on the actual work order. The AD was complied with. The aircraft was fine. But the digital traceability chain was broken, and the FSDO inspector found it on surveillance.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: GARY
excerpt_text: Letter of Correction. No penalty, but it's in my record. *(He pulls open a desk drawer, holds up a sheet of paper, puts it back.)* I keep it there to remember that the software's job is not to help me go faster. Its job is to make sure what actually happened gets recorded in a way that survives scrutiny. If it helps me go faster as a side effect, great. But that's not the primary job.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: NADIA
excerpt_text: That's a strong framing. What would the AD compliance flow look like if the software was doing its job?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: GARY
excerpt_text: First: I wish the software would refuse to let me close a work order if there's an AD that's been opened against that work order and not resolved. Not a warning I can click through. An actual block with a clear message: "AD 2017-04-06 is recorded as applicable and has no compliance record. Resolve or document inapplicability before closing." Right now, Corridor will warn you but it doesn't block. And warnings get clicked through. I've done it myself.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in compliance defensibility and audit posture and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: NADIA
excerpt_text: *(glancing at Rosa, who nods)* That's already on our list. What else?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: GARY
excerpt_text: Second: I wish the software would make it impossible for an AD compliance record to be moved or separated from its work order after it's been associated. I don't want a drag-and-drop, I don't want a copy function that silently re-links things. If an AD compliance action happened in work order number X, it should be bound to work order X for the life of the record. The only correction mechanism should be an explicit, audited amendment that requires a reason.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: NADIA
excerpt_text: Understood. Can I ask about task card workflow? How do your techs work through a 100-hour today?
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: GARY
excerpt_text: Task cards live in Corridor as a checklist attached to the work order. Each card has steps. The tech works through the steps — physically does the work, marks each step complete in Corridor, and adds any notes. If they find a discrepancy — cracked part, failed inspection point — they write it up as a discrepancy on the work order. Everything gets signed with their certificate number when they close the card.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: NADIA
excerpt_text: How do you feel about PIN-based re-authentication for signing?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: GARY
excerpt_text: *(leaning forward slightly)* Like a point-of-sale terminal?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: NADIA
excerpt_text: More like a step-up authentication event. You're logged in as yourself, but before a signing action, you re-enter your credential — PIN or password — and the system cryptographically binds that event to the record in the same database transaction as the sign-off.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for data integrity and traceability architecture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: GARY
excerpt_text: That's — yeah. That's actually what it should be. The signature should be an event, not a field. The fact that you entered your credential at that moment should be recorded and linked. Not just "Gary Hutchins, 2/22/26." That's just text. *(He looks at Rosa.)* Is that what Marcus was pushing for?
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in data integrity and traceability architecture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: NADIA
excerpt_text: Walk me through your pre-release process. What happens in the last hour before an aircraft goes back to an owner?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: GARY
excerpt_text: *(pointing to the laminated checklist on the wall)* That. Every time. I check all task cards — not just that the status in the software says complete, I look at whether every step has a sign-off with a date and cert number. I check the maintenance record — is it created, is it signed, does the aircraft total time in the software match the logbook entry. I check the AD compliance record. I check the discrepancy log — everything either corrected with a maintenance record, or deferred with owner notification documented. Then I walk the aircraft physically. Cowlings on, no tools left inside, nothing hanging.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in compliance defensibility and audit posture, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: NADIA
excerpt_text: That's essentially what we've built. We call it the Close Readiness Report.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: GARY
excerpt_text: *(pause)* Does it block closure if anything's red?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: NADIA
excerpt_text: Yes. Hard block on the Return-to-Service authorization if any precondition fails.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: GARY
excerpt_text: Then you've solved the main problem. The rest is details.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in market/backstory and mission origin that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: NADIA
excerpt_text: Last question — what would make you not trust this software? What would you watch for?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for adoption signals and trust transfer dynamics and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: GARY
excerpt_text: Three things. One: if the records aren't immutable. If someone can go back and edit a signed maintenance record without creating an audited amendment, I don't care how good the rest of it is — that's a records integrity problem and I won't use it. Two: if the system can go down and take my records with it. If it's cloud-hosted, I want to know there's a backup I can put my hands on. Physical. Not "it's in the cloud." Three: if it's designed so that the UI makes things look done when the regulatory requirement isn't actually complete. If there's a green checkmark before the FAA would say it's done, that checkmark is a liability.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in compliance defensibility and audit posture that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
speaker: NADIA
excerpt_text: Those are fair tests. We've thought about all three.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins > Requirements Extracted — Gary Hutchins Interview > REQ-GH-01: Hard Block on AD-Open Work Order Close
excerpt_text: REQ-GH-01: Hard Block on AD-Open Work Order Close Work orders with associated ADs that have no compliance record (or no documented inapplicability determination) must be blocked from closure. Warning dialogs are insufficient. Error message must name the specific AD(s) and provide a direct action path to the AD compliance record. *"Not a warning I can click through. An actual block."*
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in compliance defensibility and audit posture and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins > Requirements Extracted — Gary Hutchins Interview > REQ-GH-02: Immutable AD-to-Work-Order Binding
excerpt_text: REQ-GH-02: Immutable AD-to-Work-Order Binding Once an AD compliance record is associated with a work order, that association must be permanent and audited. No copy, drag, or template operation may silently re-link or orphan an AD compliance record. Any amendment to the association requires an explicit action with a documented reason, a separate audit log entry, and cannot be performed by the user who created the original entry without supervisor approval. *"If an AD compliance action happened in work order number X, it should be bound to work order X for the life of the record."*
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in compliance defensibility and audit posture and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins > Requirements Extracted — Gary Hutchins Interview > REQ-GH-03: Pre-Release Readiness View (Single Screen)
excerpt_text: REQ-GH-03: Pre-Release Readiness View (Single Screen) Before aircraft release, the system must present a unified readiness dashboard showing: (1) task card completion status (step-level, not card-level), (2) maintenance record existence and signature status, (3) aircraft total time reconciliation (system vs. logbook entry), (4) AD compliance current status (no overdue recurring items), (5) discrepancy log (all open items either corrected or deferred with owner documentation). Each item must show green/red state with direct navigation to the blocking record. Closure must be blocked until all items are green. *"One screen, one checklist, green or red for each item."*
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in product capability maturity (current state) and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins > Requirements Extracted — Gary Hutchins Interview > REQ-GH-04: Signing as a Cryptographic Event
excerpt_text: REQ-GH-04: Signing as a Cryptographic Event Step sign-off must not be implemented as a text field entry. Signing must require a re-authentication event (PIN or credential step-up) that is cryptographically consumed in the same database transaction as the sign-off record. The system must be able to demonstrate, to an FAA inspector, that the person who signed was authenticated at the moment of signing. *(Confirmed as already implemented — REQ-GH-04 is MET in current Athelon build.)*
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins > Requirements Extracted — Gary Hutchins Interview > REQ-GH-05: Active-First Default Dashboard
excerpt_text: REQ-GH-05: Active-First Default Dashboard The work order dashboard must default to showing "Active" work orders on first load and on each morning session. No filter selection required. This is a day-one UX requirement, not a nice-to-have. *(Confirmed as already designed — REQ-GH-05 is MET per Nadia's competitive design notes.)*
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
excerpt_text: **NADIA:** Walk me through your pre-release process. What happens in the last hour before an aircraft goes back to an owner?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
excerpt_text: **GARY:** *(pointing to the laminated checklist on the wall)* That. Every time. I check all task cards — not just that the status in the software says complete, I look at whether every step has a sign-off with a date and cert number. I check the maintenance record — is it created, is it signed, does the aircraft total time in the software match the logbook entry. I check the AD compliance record. I check the discrepancy log — everything either corrected with a maintenance record, or deferred with owner notification documented. Then I walk the aircraft physically. Cowlings on, no tools left inside, nothing hanging.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in compliance defensibility and audit posture, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
excerpt_text: The software's job in this process is to show me those five things clearly on one screen. Right now in Corridor I have to open four different tabs to see all of it. I wish the software would give me a pre-release readiness view — one screen, one checklist, green or red for each item, with the ability to click into any red item directly from that view. That would save me fifteen minutes per aircraft release and reduce the chance I miss something.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins
excerpt_text: **GARY:** Three things. One: if the records aren't immutable. If someone can go back and edit a signed maintenance record without creating an audited amendment, I don't care how good the rest of it is — that's a records integrity problem and I won't use it. Two: if the system can go down and take my records with it. If it's cloud-hosted, I want to know there's a backup I can put my hands on. Physical. Not "it's in the cloud." Three: if it's designed so that the UI makes things look done when the regulatory requirement isn't actually complete. If there's a green checkmark before the FAA would say it's done, that checkmark is a liability.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in compliance defensibility and audit posture that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins > Requirements Extracted — Gary Hutchins Interview > REQ-GH-01: Hard Block on AD-Open Work Order Close
excerpt_text: Work orders with associated ADs that have no compliance record (or no documented inapplicability determination) must be blocked from closure. Warning dialogs are insufficient. Error message must name the specific AD(s) and provide a direct action path to the AD compliance record.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in compliance defensibility and audit posture and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins > Requirements Extracted — Gary Hutchins Interview > REQ-GH-02: Immutable AD-to-Work-Order Binding
excerpt_text: Once an AD compliance record is associated with a work order, that association must be permanent and audited. No copy, drag, or template operation may silently re-link or orphan an AD compliance record. Any amendment to the association requires an explicit action with a documented reason, a separate audit log entry, and cannot be performed by the user who created the original entry without supervisor approval.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in compliance defensibility and audit posture and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Interview Transcript — Gary "Hatch" Hutchins > Requirements Extracted — Gary Hutchins Interview > REQ-GH-03: Pre-Release Readiness View (Single Screen)

section: Gary "Hatch" Hutchins — Director of Maintenance > Background
excerpt_text: Gary Hutchins has been working on airplanes since he was nineteen years old, starting as a line tech at a Piper service center in Wichita, Kansas where he ran safety wire and changed oil until his fingers bled. He earned his A&P in 1998, worked powerplant at a regional turbine shop in Tulsa for five years, and spent three years as a lead tech at a fixed-wing avionics shop before getting his IA and being pulled into a supervisory role at thirty-one. He did not plan to become a DOM. He was handed the job because the previous DOM retired suddenly and the owner needed someone who knew every airplane in the hangar by its squawks. Gary knew them.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for field workflow realities (mobile/offline/hangar constraints) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

Current complement: 12 technicians (7 A&P, 3 A&P/IA, 2 trainee), 1 QCM (Linda Paredes), 1 parts clerk, and Gary. They run roughly 600–700 work orders per year, predominantly 100-hour and annual inspections on owner-flown singles, with a growing turboprop maintenance contract for a charter operator out of Nashville.

section: Gary "Hatch" Hutchins — Director of Maintenance > The Software Incident (Recordkeeping Finding)
excerpt_text: In 2019, during a routine Part 145 surveillance inspection, an FAA FSDO inspector reviewed three work orders selected at random. One of them — a 100-hour inspection on a Piper Seneca — had an AD compliance notation that was attached to the wrong work order in Corridor. The AD (AD 2017-04-06, spar strap inspection) had been complied with correctly and was documented correctly in the paper logbook. But in Corridor, the note had been dragged to a different work order during a template copy operation that Gary had done to speed up a recurring customer's paperwork.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Gary "Hatch" Hutchins — Director of Maintenance > The Software Incident (Recordkeeping Finding)
excerpt_text: The AD itself was complied with. The aircraft was airworthy. But the FAA record in the system was in the wrong work order, which meant the digital traceability chain from the maintenance action to the AD compliance record was broken. The inspector flagged it as a recordkeeping discrepancy under 14 CFR 43.9. It did not result in a civil penalty, but it went into the station's inspection record and required a Letter of Correction.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Gary "Hatch" Hutchins — Director of Maintenance > The Software Incident (Recordkeeping Finding)
excerpt_text: Gary has not used Corridor's template copy function since. He considers this an indictment of the software design, not just his own error: *"A system that lets you accidentally detach an AD compliance note from its work order and doesn't warn you — that's not a design feature, that's a trap."*
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in compliance defensibility and audit posture, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Background
excerpt_text: Dale Renfrow was born in Farmington, New Mexico, grew up watching Lear 24s and Navajo Chieftains work the Four Corners oil patch, and decided aviation maintenance was a career before he had any business making that call. He worked summers at a Farmington FBO through high school — marshaling, fueling, cleaning exhaust stains off belly panels — and enrolled in the A&P program at San Juan College the fall after graduation. He got both ratings at 23, spent three years doing line maintenance on piston singles and light twins, and then took a job at a charter operation in Grand Junction where the fleet was King Airs and the margin for error was thinner.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Background
excerpt_text: He's been in Grand Junction ever since. Twenty-four years. Mesa Ridge is a small shop — twelve mechanics, two full-time IA holders, a DOM who has been there longer than the building's current HVAC system. They do annuals on everything from student-trainer 172s to the occasional SF50 that the resort crowd brings in from Aspen and Telluride. King Airs are the bread and butter. Dale has done enough C90 annuals that he can tell you, without looking, exactly which access panels will have fasteners that require a #3 Phillips instead of the #2 the maintenance manual insists on. He's wrong about very little on a C90.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in risk landscape and failure modes that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Background
excerpt_text: He applied for his IA in 2011 after his DOM retired and the shop was left without sign-off authority for three weeks while FAASTeam paperwork moved through Denver FSDO. He passed the oral on first attempt, took the written two days later, and has held the authorization continuously since. He's had one near-renewal scare — a family medical event in 2019 put him out of the shop for six weeks, and he came back to find he'd missed the 90-day active inspection requirement by twelve days. He submitted a letter of explanation to FSDO, provided documentation, and it was resolved without action. He does not talk about that period lightly, and he does not miss deadlines now.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in compliance defensibility and audit posture, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Background
excerpt_text: The IA means something different to him than it does to people who see it as a credential. It is personal exposure. Every return-to-service he signs has his name on it in a federal record. If something goes wrong on an aircraft he released, the first thing investigators pull is the maintenance record, and the first name on that record is his. He is aware of this every time he picks up a pen. He will be aware of it every time he places a digital signature.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Background
excerpt_text: He agreed to the Athelon embed because the DOM asked him to and because, when he looked at their workflow mockups, he had specific objections he wanted to be on record as having raised. He said it exactly that way: "on record." He means it.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Background
excerpt_text: He got his private certificate in 2008, after fifteen years of telling himself he'd get around to it. He says the certificate made him a better IA because he now understands what it feels like to be the person at the other end of the maintenance record — the person who is about to climb into the aircraft that Dale signed off and fly it somewhere. He doesn't think every IA should be a pilot. He thinks every IA should have thought about that feeling long and hard. He has. He thinks about it every time he signs a return-to-service.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in market/backstory and mission origin, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > The IA Renewal Cycle and What It Means to Him
excerpt_text: Dale renews his IA biennially through Denver FSDO. The renewal requires passing a written test, demonstrating that he's performed annual inspections in the preceding 12 months, and appearing in person at the district office. He tracks his renewal date the way some people track mortgage payments — on a wall calendar in the shop, in his phone, and in a small notebook he keeps in his tool chest. He has never been late. After the 2019 close call, he also tracks the 90-day active inspection requirement on a rolling basis. If he's three months from a slow period and getting close to the edge, he looks for annuals to take on specifically to maintain currency. This is not paranoia. This is the job.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in market/backstory and mission origin, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > The IA Renewal Cycle and What It Means to Him
excerpt_text: The biennial written test covers regulations, inspection procedures, airworthiness standards, and maintenance record requirements. Dale studies for it even though he knows the material. His reasoning: "If I'm not willing to study the regulations before signing my name under them, why am I signing my name under them?" He scored 96 on his most recent renewal. He mentions this not to brag but to establish context — he is not guessing at what the regulations require. He knows.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > The IA Renewal Cycle and What It Means to Him
excerpt_text: The renewal cycle also makes him sensitive to any system that might create uncertainty about whether his IA is current at the time of a sign-off. He wants Athelon's system to verify IA currency before allowing the sign-off workflow to open. Not as a courtesy check. As a hard gate. If his IA number is expired in the system, the workflow should stop and tell him, not let him complete a sign-off that will later be questioned.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in product capability maturity (current state), suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Aircraft He Regularly Inspects
excerpt_text: Dale's annual inspection volume runs roughly 40–55 aircraft per year, which puts him near the high end for a shop Mesa Ridge's size. The distribution matters:
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Aircraft He Regularly Inspects
excerpt_text: **Cessna singles (172, 182, T210):** His volume baseline. He can perform a 172 annual in his sleep. He does not perform them in his sleep. This is a distinction he makes explicitly: familiarity is not permission to stop looking. He has found corrosion in a 172 wing spar that the previous two annual inspectors missed. The aircraft had 2,200 hours on it. Nobody was looking at that panel.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Aircraft He Regularly Inspects
excerpt_text: **Beechcraft Bonanza/Baron:** The Bonanza is his favorite aircraft to inspect and the one he trusts least. The V-tail variants especially. He has strong opinions about the corrosion-prone area behind the horizontal stabilizer carry-through, the fuel selector valve that mechanics check wrong half the time, and the gear actuator that will fail in a specific and predictable way if the previous inspector didn't torque the jam nut correctly. He finds something on almost every Bonanza he opens. This does not comfort him.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Aircraft He Regularly Inspects
excerpt_text: **King Air C90/B200:** Mesa Ridge's primary commercial business. Dale has done enough of these that he has a personal checklist that supplements the Beechcraft maintenance manual, with annotations based on things he's found that the factory didn't anticipate. He keeps it in a binder. He has not digitized it. He knows what that says about him. He also knows the King Air is the aircraft most likely to produce a maintenance record that gets scrutinized by an FSDO, because Part 135 operators fly King Airs for hire, and their records get pulled on a different schedule than private owner aircraft. His King Air record entries are longer and more specific than any other aircraft type in his annual volume. This is intentional.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in compliance defensibility and audit posture that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Aircraft He Regularly Inspects
excerpt_text: **Cirrus SR22 / SF50 Vision Jet:** The SF50 is the aircraft he is most cautious about. It's a jet. Mesa Ridge is piston/turboprop rated, and the SF50 comes in under their authorization, but it is a different machine. He reviews his notes before every SF50 annual. He calls the Cirrus tech line more than he would like to admit. He has told the DOM he would prefer to develop a formal supplemental inspection checklist for the SF50 before they accept another one. The DOM has agreed in principle. Dale is drafting it. It is twelve pages so far and not finished.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for operational/governance discipline and controls and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Paper vs. Software History
excerpt_text: Dale's paper history is uncomplicated: he was trained on paper, worked on paper for seventeen years, and considers paper the devil he knows. His shop adopted an MRO system called AviWatch in 2018. The implementation was handled by a consultant who left before the configuration was finished. The result was a system that required paper backup on every annual because three of the inspection form fields didn't map to what the FAA actually required on a maintenance record. They used AviWatch and paper in parallel for two years. When the consultant's work finally got corrected and re-configured, it took Dale three days to audit two years of dual records to confirm they were consistent. He found four discrepancies. None were safety-critical. All were documentation errors. He does not consider documentation errors trivial.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Paper vs. Software History
excerpt_text: AviWatch has been stable since the fix. Dale now uses it for scheduling, parts tracking, and work order management. He does not use it for IA sign-offs. When it's time to sign an annual, he prints the inspection checklist summary, reviews it, signs with a pen, and the DOM scans it. That workflow is, by his own admission, absurd. He knows it. He wants something better. He does not yet trust that what he's been shown is better.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in adoption signals and trust transfer dynamics, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Paper vs. Software History
excerpt_text: He has also seen the industry shift toward fully digital annuals from the outside — colleagues at larger Part 145 shops who've adopted integrated MRO platforms and moved all IA sign-offs into the software. Some of them like it. None of them have given him a clean answer when he's asked: "What happens if the system generates an incorrect record entry and you didn't catch it before sign-off?" The most common answer is "that hasn't happened." He considers that a non-answer. He will ask the same question to Athelon.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Paper vs. Software History
excerpt_text: He has seen two other MRO products during vendor demos over the years. Both were excluded from serious consideration — one because it had no mechanism to capture the specific regulatory citation required on a return-to-service entry, and one because the IA sign-off confirmation screen did not display the aircraft N-number at the moment of signature. He rejected that second one in the demo. The sales rep thought he was being difficult. He was not being difficult. He was reading 14 CFR Part 43.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > What a Legally Defensible Digital IA Sign-Off Requires
excerpt_text: Dale has a list. It is not hypothetical. He drafted it the week before the Athelon embed began, typed it in his shop's break room on a Thursday evening, and printed two copies — one for the meeting and one for his own records. He calls it his "43.9 checklist" because that's the regulation it maps to.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > What a Legally Defensible Digital IA Sign-Off Requires
excerpt_text: 14 CFR §43.9 specifies what a maintenance record entry must contain: description of work performed, date of completion, name and certificate number of the person who performed the work, the person's signature, and (for IA sign-offs) the IA number. Dale considers each of these non-negotiable fields in any digital system. He will add several more.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > What a Legally Defensible Digital IA Sign-Off Requires
excerpt_text: 1. **The sign-off screen must display, before confirmation:** aircraft N-number, aircraft make/model/serial, the specific inspection item being certified, the regulatory basis for the sign-off (annual under §91.409, or return-to-service under §43.7, stated explicitly), the date, his name, his A&P certificate number, and his IA number. Not after. Before. He needs to be reading what he is certifying, not what was certified.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > What a Legally Defensible Digital IA Sign-Off Requires
excerpt_text: 2. **The signature action must be atomic and immediately confirmed.** When he completes the authentication step, the system must display the completed record within two seconds. Not "submitted." Not "processing." The record, with his information on it, the way it will appear to an FAA inspector. If there is a spinner, he will treat the sign-off as incomplete until he sees the record.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > What a Legally Defensible Digital IA Sign-Off Requires
excerpt_text: 3. **The system must generate a record entry that satisfies §43.9 in its stored format**, not just in the UI. He wants to see the actual stored record — not a prettified view — at least once during evaluation. He wants to know what an FSDO would see if they pulled the record through a data export. He has asked this at every vendor demo. Nobody has ever shown him without significant follow-up.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > What a Legally Defensible Digital IA Sign-Off Requires
excerpt_text: 4. **The completed IA entry must be immutable.** He cannot sign off an annual and have the record be editable afterward without a documented amendment trail. If someone changes a field after his signature, there must be an audit record, a re-signing requirement, and a notification to him. Non-negotiable.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in compliance defensibility and audit posture and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > What a Legally Defensible Digital IA Sign-Off Requires
excerpt_text: 5. **He must be able to export a printed record at any time** that looks like a traditional maintenance log entry — plain language, his identifying information, the work described, the signature indicator. He works with FSDO inspectors who are in their late fifties and will not navigate a software portal during a ramp inspection. The paper has to be available.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His FSDO History and What It Shaped
excerpt_text: Dale has had three formal interactions with FAA inspectors beyond routine renewals. None resulted in enforcement action. All of them shaped him.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His FSDO History and What It Shaped
excerpt_text: The first was a ramp check in 2013 on a Bonanza he had just returned to service. The inspector found the maintenance record entry acceptable but asked Dale to walk through his inspection methodology on the landing gear. The inspector knew more about Bonanza gear than Dale expected, asked questions Dale couldn't answer on the spot about one of the actuator tolerances, and told him to look it up before signing the next one. Dale looked it up. He still has the maintenance manual page, annotated, in the Bonanza section of his reference binder. He is not embarrassed about this. "She was right. I was close, but close isn't the tolerance."
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in compliance defensibility and audit posture that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His FSDO History and What It Shaped
excerpt_text: The second was a complaint investigation in 2016. A customer disputed that the maintenance performed during an annual had addressed a previously noted squawk. The dispute came down to the paper record — specifically, whether a finding noted in informal shop shorthand in the previous year's record was the same item the customer was claiming was missed. The record was ambiguous. Dale spent two weeks in close contact with the FSDO coordinating documentation. The investigation was closed with no finding against him, but the lesson was permanent: informal documentation language creates risk. If the record says "checked and serviceable," that means nothing in an investigation. "Inspected per AMM 27-00-00, found within limits, no further action required" means something.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in risk landscape and failure modes and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His FSDO History and What It Shaped
excerpt_text: He writes his maintenance record entries in complete sentences. He has written them that way since 2016. He will expect the same of any system that generates record language on his behalf — and he considers auto-generated record text a significant risk unless he can review and edit it before it's committed under his signature.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in data integrity and traceability architecture that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His FSDO History and What It Shaped
excerpt_text: The third interaction was a 2022 notification from Denver FSDO that they were conducting a survey of IA holders on their familiarity with AC 120-78B, the electronic recordkeeping advisory circular. Dale completed the survey, attached three pages of written comments, and received a form acknowledgment. He does not know if anyone read his comments. He has a copy.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in data integrity and traceability architecture, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His Position on Re-Authentication as Proxy for Wet Signature
excerpt_text: He is not opposed to PIN entry or biometrics as a signature mechanism. He understands the FAA has accepted electronic signatures in maintenance records, and he has read AC 120-78B, which is the advisory circular that covers electronic recordkeeping systems. He knows what the circular requires: that the electronic signature uniquely identifies the individual, that there are controls to prevent unauthorized use, and that the system can authenticate records if questioned. He can cite these requirements from memory, and he will.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His Position on Re-Authentication as Proxy for Wet Signature
excerpt_text: The wet signature is not valuable because ink is permanent. It is valuable because it is a deliberate physical act that cannot be performed accidentally or distractedly. You have to pick up the pen, find the line, and make the mark. That sequence imposes a pause. That pause is where mistakes get caught. He has caught his own errors in that pause. He has caught other people's errors in that pause. He views that pause as load-bearing.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in data integrity and traceability architecture and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His Position on Re-Authentication as Proxy for Wet Signature
excerpt_text: A PIN can be entered reflexively. A thumbprint can be captured passively. He does not believe biometrics, as typically implemented in commercial software, provide the same forcing function as a physical signature. He wants the system to engineer that pause back into the flow.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for data integrity and traceability architecture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His Position on Re-Authentication as Proxy for Wet Signature
excerpt_text: Display a full summary of what he is certifying before he enters credentials — not a button that says "Review and Sign" but the actual certification statement, the specific regulatory language, in full, with his name and IA number already populated. He reads it. Then he signs.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for data integrity and traceability architecture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His Position on Re-Authentication as Proxy for Wet Signature
excerpt_text: Require a PIN, not biometrics alone. He mistrusts fingerprint on a shop tablet that has been handled by twelve mechanics. He also mistrusts the failure modes — a biometric failure at the wrong moment and suddenly the aircraft is sitting on the ramp with a return-to-service that didn't complete. A PIN is more predictable.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in adoption signals and trust transfer dynamics that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His Position on Re-Authentication as Proxy for Wet Signature
excerpt_text: Make the step impossible to skip or abbreviate. No "remember this device for 30 days." No session token that keeps him authenticated across sign-offs. Every IA signature is a fresh authentication. Every time.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for data integrity and traceability architecture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His Position on Re-Authentication as Proxy for Wet Signature
excerpt_text: Log the authentication event separately from the record entry, so that if the signature is ever questioned, there are two independent data points: the record, and the authentication log with timestamp and IP or device identifier.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for data integrity and traceability architecture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His Position on Re-Authentication as Proxy for Wet Signature
excerpt_text: He has been asked, in a previous vendor demo, whether this level of scrutiny is "a little much" for a software sign-off. He said: "The last thing I signed was a return-to-service on a King Air that was about to carry six people to Denver in January. You tell me."
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for data integrity and traceability architecture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > What a Successful Athelon Evaluation Looks Like to Him
excerpt_text: Before he will use the Athelon sign-off workflow on a live annual, he wants to run one complete simulated annual end-to-end: open a work order, complete an inspection checklist, trigger the IA sign-off flow, authenticate, view the committed record, export a printed copy, and then have someone else — ideally someone from Athelon's engineering team who has never done the scenario before — open the same record in the system and find every required data field without his guidance. If they can find his IA number, the date, the regulatory citation, the description of work, and his authentication event log within three minutes of first looking at the record, the system passes the readability test.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in product capability maturity (current state), suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > What a Successful Athelon Evaluation Looks Like to Him
excerpt_text: He also wants to deliberately trigger an edge case: start an IA sign-off, interrupt it mid-flow (kill the network connection, close the browser tab, whatever the system allows), and see what happens. Does the system think the record is complete? Partial? Unknown? He wants the answer to be unambiguous. He has a term for systems that return an ambiguous state after an interrupted workflow: "liability buckets." He will not sign into a liability bucket.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > What a Successful Athelon Evaluation Looks Like to Him
excerpt_text: He will not sign a live return-to-service on an Athelon-managed record until he has seen the system survive that interrupted-workflow test cleanly at least twice. He is willing to be the person who runs the test. He is not willing to skip it.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > What a Successful Athelon Evaluation Looks Like to Him
excerpt_text: If Athelon passes his evaluation, he will say so explicitly, in writing, to the DOM and to the Athelon project lead. He considers written endorsement from a working IA to be something Athelon should want and is willing to provide it if it's earned. He's said this once, in the intake call, and didn't repeat it. He doesn't need to.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in future-state roadmap and expansion vectors, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Personality & Interview Notes
excerpt_text: Quiet. Precise. The kind of person who pauses before answering not because he's uncertain but because he's editing. His answers come out complete — subject, verb, necessary modifiers — and then he stops. He does not add "you know?" He does not trail off. When he's done with a point, he's done.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Personality & Interview Notes
excerpt_text: He has been married for nineteen years. His wife is an ER nurse. They have two teenagers and a 1975 Cessna 182 that he and a college friend co-own out of Walker Field. He flies when the weather is good, the kids aren't in a tournament, and the 182 isn't waiting on a part. These conditions converge less often than he'd prefer.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for operational/governance discipline and controls and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Personality & Interview Notes
excerpt_text: He does not talk about his IA very much in casual conversation. Other mechanics will mention theirs the way people mention advanced degrees — as a credential marker. Dale does not do this. He considers it a description of his liability profile, not his status, and he is not interested in advertising his liability profile to strangers. If you ask him directly whether he holds an IA, he will answer. He will not elaborate unless the question is substantive. This is one of the things that makes him effective in an embed role — he is not here to protect the credential. He is here to protect what it represents.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Personality & Interview Notes
excerpt_text: He is not easy to read as warm or cold. He is neither. He is focused. He has been doing an exacting, liability-dense job for twenty-four years and he has developed the personality appropriate to it. He will not give Athelon the benefit of the doubt as a default. He will give them credit for specific things when they earn it. He told the Athelon PM in the intake call: "I've seen a lot of software built by people who've never been at an aircraft at seven in the morning trying to figure out if they can put it back in service. I'm here to help you not be that." He meant it as an offer. It came out as a warning. He's aware of this and considers it fine.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in product capability maturity (current state) that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Personality & Interview Notes
excerpt_text: He is not a luddite. He texts from his phone, uses GPS, files IFR flight plans through ForeFlight (he has a private certificate, 340 hours, flies a 182 he co-owns with a friend). He has nothing against technology. He has something against technology that was built without understanding what happens when it fails while someone's name is on a federal document.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Personality & Interview Notes
excerpt_text: He will ask hard questions in the interview. They will be fair. He will listen to the answers. He will update his position if the answers are good. He has not yet seen a vendor answer all of his questions well. He is willing to be the first one.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for future-state roadmap and expansion vectors and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Personality & Interview Notes
excerpt_text: One last note for interviewers: do not tell him the system is "compliant" without being prepared to say compliant with what, and specifically how. He has heard "compliant with FAA recordkeeping requirements" used to describe systems that were not. He will ask for the citation. Have it.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His Relationship with the A&P Mechanics on the Team
excerpt_text: Dale has a specific dynamic with A&Ps who work below him in the sign-off chain, and it is worth understanding before anyone puts him in a room with Troy Weaver or the other mechanics.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for data integrity and traceability architecture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His Relationship with the A&P Mechanics on the Team
excerpt_text: He is not a gatekeeper by personality, but he is one by function. When he signs the return-to-service, he is certifying that the work the mechanics performed was done correctly and that the aircraft meets its type certificate. If the mechanic's work was wrong, his signature is still on the record. He has internalized this to the degree that he reviews task cards with a different eye than most A&Ps expect. He is not re-doing the work. He is verifying that the documentation of the work matches what an inspector would need to see — that the correct AMM revision was referenced, that the tolerances cited match the actual findings, that nothing was signed off with "checked OK" when the regulation requires a measured value.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His Relationship with the A&P Mechanics on the Team
excerpt_text: This creates occasional friction. Mechanics who are confident in their work sometimes experience his review process as distrust. He does not experience it as distrust. He experiences it as doing his job. He will say this directly, once, if a mechanic expresses frustration: "I trust you did the work right. This is about whether we can prove it." He does not repeat it.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for adoption signals and trust transfer dynamics and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His Relationship with the A&P Mechanics on the Team
excerpt_text: He will expect the Athelon system to make his review role explicit and workable — that is, he needs a view into a task card that shows him the mechanic's sign-off, the referenced document, the findings entered, and any notes, all in one place, before he initiates his own sign-off. He will not move to IA sign-off from a summary screen that doesn't show him the underlying work. That is not how an annual inspection works, and he will say so.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for operational/governance discipline and controls and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > His Relationship with the A&P Mechanics on the Team
excerpt_text: One thing he does not want: a system where his IA status is visually prominent in the workflow as a status indicator or dashboard element. He has used systems that have an "IA sign-off pending" banner with his name on it, visible to everyone in the shop, counting down. He finds this creates pressure that has no place in the sign-off decision. The readiness of an aircraft for return to service is not a queue item to be cleared. He will make this point in the interview. He expects the design team to hear it.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: Sign-off screen must display full certification statement (with regulatory citation) **before** credential entry — not after, not as a confirmation, before
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in future-state roadmap and expansion vectors, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: Authentication must be per-signature, not per-session; no persistent login carries across an IA sign-off
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in data integrity and traceability architecture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: PIN required for IA sign-off; biometrics alone are not acceptable at this time
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for future-state roadmap and expansion vectors and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: Completed IA record must be immediately viewable in its stored format, not a UI proxy
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in future-state roadmap and expansion vectors, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: Post-signature record must be immutable; any amendment requires documented trail and re-sign
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in data integrity and traceability architecture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: Audit log for authentication events must be independent of and linkable to the record entry
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: Export to printed maintenance record must be available at any time and must satisfy §43.9 on its face
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in future-state roadmap and expansion vectors, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: System must handle offline / interrupted sign-off gracefully: if authentication completed but record write failed, he needs to know, not find out during an audit
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: He wants to see the actual data schema for a completed IA maintenance entry — not a demo, not a screenshot — before he approves the sign-off flow for production use
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for data integrity and traceability architecture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: The IA number field is mandatory and must appear in the stored record. He has seen two systems that stored the certificate number but not the IA number as a separate field. Both were rejected.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in future-state roadmap and expansion vectors, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: Before IA sign-off workflow opens, the system must verify IA currency — hard gate, not a soft warning. If his IA is not current in the system, the workflow stops.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in product capability maturity (current state) and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: The IA review view must surface: mechanic's task-level sign-off, referenced AMM/document with revision, measured findings where required, and any notes — all before Dale initiates his own sign-off. No sign-off from a summary page that doesn't show underlying work.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in operational/governance discipline and controls, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: Auto-generated record language must be reviewable and editable by Dale before commitment. He will not certify record text he has not read and approved. Any system that generates and commits record text in a single step is rejected on that basis alone.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in operational/governance discipline and controls, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: Interrupted workflow (network drop, session timeout, browser close) must resolve to a clearly documented incomplete state with no ambiguity about whether a record was committed. He will test this deliberately before going live.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in product capability maturity (current state), suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: He wants a written statement from Athelon describing how the system satisfies AC 120-78B, specifically sections 4 and 5. Not a marketing claim. A technical statement he can attach to his own file.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: The IA sign-off view must not surface as a workflow pressure indicator (no "IA pending" dashboards with his name attached). The return-to-service decision is his call on his timeline, not a queue item.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in product capability maturity (current state), suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Dale Renfrow — IA Holder (Inspection Authorization) > Known Requirements He Will Surface in Interview
excerpt_text: He will request a read-only test account with access to a completed sample record before any live use. He will review that sample record against §43.9 manually before signing anything under his IA.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for operational/governance discipline and controls and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.
