section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Background
excerpt_text: Renata grew up in El Paso, and the first airplane she ever touched was a battered Cessna 172 at a charter outfit where her uncle worked line. She was fifteen. She didn't go to aviation school immediately — she spent two years at a community college studying general mechanics and picking up Spanish-language aircraft maintenance manuals her uncle brought home, before she transferred to a dedicated A&P program in Tucson. She got her certificate at twenty-three, two years later than most of her cohort, and she has never once considered that a disadvantage. She says she learned to read technical documentation as a skill, not just a habit, because she had to translate it in her head before the English version made the same sense.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for future-state roadmap and expansion vectors and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Background
excerpt_text: Her first eight years were hands-on production: turboprops at a King Air specialty shop in Wichita called Mesa Air Services (no relation to the carrier), then four years at Hawker Beechcraft's in-house service network before they reorganized out of existence. She did structural repairs, progressive inspections, engine changes, prop overhauls. She got her IA in her tenth year because the chief inspector — a man named Dave Krakowski who she describes as "precise and unkind in the most useful possible way" — pushed her to take it. Krakowski told her she argued about records like an inspector, not a mechanic, and that she was wasting everyone's time staying on the floor.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Background
excerpt_text: She moved to inspection at thirty-one, under Krakowski at Mesa Air, and found that her instincts about documentation — the thing that had made her slightly annoying to supervisors as a mechanic — were exactly the instincts you needed in QC. She became the QCM at Mesa Air when Krakowski retired in 2017. She's been the QCM at TriState since 2020, when Mesa Air lost a major contract and downsized.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Background
excerpt_text: She has worked with the following MRO systems across her career: a legacy CAMP maintenance tracking install used purely for tracking (not workflow), a full-lifecycle deployment of AVMRO Pro (which she calls "almost good"), a home-built FileMaker database at Mesa that was kept alive on life support by one contract IT guy, and Ramco Aviation at TriState. She has opinions about all of them, but the one she returns to unprompted is AVMRO Pro — specifically its discrepancy tracking module, which she considers a near-miss-level failure dressed up in professional packaging.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in product capability maturity (current state) that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Background
excerpt_text: She agreed to embed with the Athelon team because she reviewed their stated approach to audit trail architecture and thought it sounded like someone had actually talked to a QCM before writing the spec. She is cautiously willing to be proven right.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Her Relationship with the DOM
excerpt_text: This is a thing she has thought about carefully, and she'll tell you about it if you ask directly. If you don't ask, she won't bring it up, but you'll notice the structure of how she talks about her role makes it visible anyway.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for operational/governance discipline and controls and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Her Relationship with the DOM
excerpt_text: At TriState the DOM is a man named Marcus Webb — competent, experienced, primarily focused on production efficiency and customer turnaround. Renata respects him. They do not always agree. The organizational structure is explicit: she reports to the Accountable Manager, not to Marcus, and Marcus cannot direct her inspection and quality functions. This is not a political arrangement. It is a regulatory requirement. Part 145 requires that the quality function have independence from production management, and that independence only means something if it can survive a disagreement.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in compliance defensibility and audit posture and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Her Relationship with the DOM
excerpt_text: She has used her independence twice in nine years as a QCM. Once at Mesa Air, where a customer was pushing for a deferral of a hydraulic anomaly that she felt did not meet the criteria for deferral under the MEL. The DOM at the time wanted to find a path that satisfied the customer. She said no. The deferral did not happen. The customer was unhappy. A more thorough inspection the following week found a failed component that would have been a serious problem at altitude.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in compliance defensibility and audit posture that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Her Relationship with the DOM
excerpt_text: The second time was at TriState. A mechanic was assigned to an inspection task requiring Citation-specific recurrent training. His training record in Ramco showed the completion. She pulled the actual training certificate from the training provider's portal as part of a random verification she does quarterly. The certificate had a different completion date than the Ramco record, and the discrepancy pushed the recurrency window. She grounded the work order until the record was corrected and re-verified. The mechanic had made an honest data-entry error. Marcus was not pleased about the delay. She documented the incident, the resolution, and the procedural revision that followed.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Her Relationship with the DOM
excerpt_text: She does not describe either of these as victories. She describes them as what the quality function is for. She will say, without drama, that a QCM who hasn't needed to use their independent authority is either in an unusually well-run shop or hasn't been looking closely enough.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Her Relationship with the DOM
excerpt_text: Her relationship with Athelon's system design is shaped by this: she needs the software to support her authority, not route around it. If the DOM can override a return-to-service gate in the system without her sign-off, the gate is decorative. She will ask about this specifically.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for operational/governance discipline and controls and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > The QCM Role as She Defines It
excerpt_text: Renata does not describe her job as "quality assurance." She describes it as **maintaining the conditions under which the mechanics can sign their names to their work without lying.** The distinction matters to her.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for operational/governance discipline and controls and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > The QCM Role as She Defines It
excerpt_text: Her authority is independent of the Director of Maintenance, and she has exercised it against the DOM twice in her career — once at Mesa Air (a deferred maintenance item she felt was improperly classified) and once at TriState (a mechanic's training record that did not reflect the actual qualification he was signing off for). In both cases, she prevailed. In both cases, the working relationship survived, though one of them was uncomfortable for about four months. She does not regard those incidents as conflicts. She regards them as the system working correctly.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > The QCM Role as She Defines It
excerpt_text: **1. The inspection system.** Who is authorized to inspect what, on what aircraft, under what limitations. This is not a spreadsheet she updates at annual review — it is a living document that must track certificate changes, IA lapses, and limitation expansions in something close to real time. If a mechanic's authorization changes and the system doesn't reflect it before the next work order, she considers that a gap.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in future-state roadmap and expansion vectors and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > The QCM Role as She Defines It
excerpt_text: **2. Return-to-service procedures.** Not just the act of signing the 8130 or the maintenance release — the entire chain of evidence that supports the signature. The task cards, the parts traceability, the inspector sign-offs, the deferred items list, the work order status. She has seen two shops (not hers) have return-to-service signatures challenged by FAA because the supporting documentation was incomplete at the time of signature, even though it was completed afterward. "Afterward doesn't count," she says. "The signature is the moment. The record has to be complete at the moment."
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > The QCM Role as She Defines It
excerpt_text: **3. Discrepancy tracking.** Every finding that isn't normal, expected maintenance needs to be captured, described, dispositioned, and closed. Not because the FAA wants it — they do — but because four years from now, when a different mechanic opens that panel and sees something odd, the only thing standing between them and a guessing game is whatever was written down. She has a near-religious view of discrepancy narrative quality. Vague dispositions make her visibly tense.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in compliance defensibility and audit posture, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > The QCM Role as She Defines It
excerpt_text: **4. Training records.** Who is qualified for what, on what aircraft type, with what recency. She has a spreadsheet she maintains in parallel with the official system because she does not trust either system she's ever used to surface expiring qualifications proactively. That parallel spreadsheet is a symptom. She knows it's a symptom. She wants it to not be necessary.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for adoption signals and trust transfer dynamics and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > The AVMRO Pro Incident
excerpt_text: AVMRO Pro kept its discrepancy tracking and its task card completion records in separate modules with a linkage field — a discrepancy ID that was supposed to tie the finding to the corrective action on the work order. The linkage worked well enough during normal operations. During an unscheduled inspection on an ERJ-145 that came in with a reported pressurization anomaly, a mechanic opened a finding on the aft pressure bulkhead that was related to, but not identical to, the reported squawk. He entered it as a sub-discrepancy under the original work order, which was technically correct per the shop's procedure.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in market/backstory and mission origin, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > The AVMRO Pro Incident
excerpt_text: The problem: the print function for the return-to-service documentation package pulled task card completions from one module and discrepancy closeouts from the other, and the linkage logic for sub-discrepancies had a known but undocumented bug. The sub-discrepancy showed as open in the discrepancy module print. The task card showed it as closed. The print package that went with the aircraft showed a discrepancy as open.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in product capability maturity (current state) that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > The AVMRO Pro Incident
excerpt_text: The aircraft was returned to service. The customer's director of maintenance noticed the discrepancy three days later. The FAA got involved. No finding was made — the corrective action had genuinely been completed, and the task card signature was legitimate — but the process of demonstrating that took two weeks, a lot of documentation reconstruction, and one extremely tense meeting with a Flight Standards District Office inspector. The software vendor's support ticket log showed the bug had been reported by two other customers eighteen months earlier.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > The AVMRO Pro Incident
excerpt_text: She has never purchased or recommended AVMRO Pro to anyone. She talks about the incident calmly, in the same way a pilot describes a serious near-miss: matter-of-fact on the surface, very precise about details, alert to anyone who seems to be minimizing it.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in operational/governance discipline and controls, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > The AVMRO Pro Incident
excerpt_text: Her requirement, which she states as a non-negotiable: **discrepancy records and task completion records must be the same record, or must be architecturally impossible to desynchronize.** She doesn't care which. She cares that she never again has to explain to an FAA inspector why two modules show different status for the same finding.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > FAA Surveillance: What She Knows
excerpt_text: TriState has been through two full FAA surveillance inspections since she became QCM (2021 and 2024), plus one targeted records review in 2022 that followed a service difficulty report on a King Air. She has also been present for inspections at Mesa Air, including one in 2016 that she considers formative.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > FAA Surveillance: What She Knows
excerpt_text: She knows the rhythm of a surveillance inspection. The FSDO inspector arrives with a sampling plan — not announced, usually within a general window — and begins with two things: the operations specifications and the repair station manual. They are not looking for perfection. They are looking for consistency between what the manual says you do and what the records show you did. If your manual says training records are reviewed quarterly and your training records were last reviewed nine months ago, the discrepancy is not about training records. It's about whether your quality system is operational.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in compliance defensibility and audit posture that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > FAA Surveillance: What She Knows
excerpt_text: The 2021 inspection at TriState ran three days. Two inspectors. They pulled seventeen work orders across the prior twelve months, asked to see training records for every mechanic whose name appeared on those work orders, reviewed the repair station manual and the ops specs in full, and walked the hangar floor. They found one observation — not a finding, an observation — about the formatting of the deferred maintenance log, which did not match the format described in the RSM. Renata had updated the log format six months earlier without updating the RSM reference. She corrected the RSM the same afternoon the inspection closed. The 2024 inspection was three days, three inspectors, and zero observations. She regards the gap between those two outcomes as the result of four years of incremental procedure improvements, not luck.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in compliance defensibility and audit posture that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > FAA Surveillance: What She Knows
excerpt_text: **Blank or vague fields.** An inspector who opens ten work orders and finds three "corrective action" fields that say "repaired" with no further detail will immediately expand the sample. She has trained her mechanics to narrate, not abbreviate.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > FAA Surveillance: What She Knows
excerpt_text: **Timestamps that don't make sense.** A sign-off timestamped before a parts receipt. A return-to-service signed before the final task card was completed. These are usually honest data-entry errors, but they look like fraud until proven otherwise, and the proving takes time she doesn't want to spend.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > FAA Surveillance: What She Knows
excerpt_text: **Broken chains of traceability.** Parts without 8130-3s, or 8130-3s that aren't linked to the specific work order. She has a parts-receipt-to-work-order linking procedure that is more elaborate than the Ramco system requires, because Ramco's default parts traceability linkage is — in her words — "adequate for an audit only if the auditor doesn't push."
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > FAA Surveillance: What She Knows
excerpt_text: **Training records that don't support the signatures on the work orders.** This is the one she watches most carefully. If a mechanic signs off a task that requires type-specific training and the training record shows that qualification lapsed two months ago, the work order is potentially invalid, and she is potentially explaining a recordkeeping failure to an inspector. She reviews qualification status before any work order is opened on a new tail type. Every time. By hand, because neither Ramco nor anything she's used before will do it automatically and reliably.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in compliance defensibility and audit posture that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > FAA Surveillance: What She Knows
excerpt_text: The 2022 records review was the closest she's come to a finding. An SDR (service difficulty report) on a King Air bleed air duct repair had been filed, but the internal corrective action documentation — the part that shows what the shop did to prevent recurrence — wasn't linked in Ramco. It existed in a separate file the director of maintenance kept. It was complete and accurate. But it wasn't in the system that the inspector was looking at. She spent four hours that afternoon in a conference room demonstrating that the documentation existed, that it had been completed on time, and that the only failure was a filing procedure gap. No finding. But she revised the corrective action filing procedure the next week, and she documented the revision.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in risk landscape and failure modes that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Digital Records vs. Paper Backup
excerpt_text: She is not opposed to digital records. She thinks digital records, when correctly implemented, are superior to paper in almost every meaningful way. They don't burn, don't flood, don't get lost in a file room move, don't become illegible when someone spills hydraulic fluid on them, and can be searched in seconds.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Digital Records vs. Paper Backup
excerpt_text: She is opposed to **unverified digital records** — systems where the existence of a record in the database is assumed to be identical to the record being correct, complete, and retrievable. She has seen three failure modes that she describes by name:
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in risk landscape and failure modes that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Digital Records vs. Paper Backup
excerpt_text: **The silent truncation.** A mechanic enters a long narrative in a free-text field. The system accepts it. The database column is defined with a character limit that nobody publicized. The last 400 characters are silently dropped. The print version looks complete. The stored version is not. She discovered this failure mode at a shop she was consulting with, not at a shop she ran. The corrective action narratives on seventeen closed work orders were incomplete. None of the mechanics knew. The system had never shown an error.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in risk landscape and failure modes that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Digital Records vs. Paper Backup
excerpt_text: **The export failure.** Regulatory records must be producible on demand. At two shops she has consulted with (not TriState), the MRO system's export function — the one that generates the documentation package for a completed work order — had degraded over time as the system was updated. The exports were incomplete in ways that weren't visible during normal operation, only when someone actually tried to use them for an inspection. In one case, part numbers were being dropped from the parts traceability section of the export. The records in the database were correct. The printed document was not. The difference was in a template file that had been overwritten by an update.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in data integrity and traceability architecture and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Digital Records vs. Paper Backup
excerpt_text: **The offline gap.** Work was performed and signed off on a tablet during a period when the shop's WiFi was down. The system said "sync pending." Nobody checked whether the sync completed. Six months later, during a records review, three task card sign-offs from that day are in the local device cache and not in the central database. This is not a hypothetical. She knows the shop. She knows the inspector who found it.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in field workflow realities (mobile/offline/hangar constraints) that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Digital Records vs. Paper Backup
excerpt_text: Her position on paper backup is pragmatic: she does not require paper duplicates of everything, which would double the records workload and introduce its own inconsistency risks. She requires that every digital record have a **verified export path** — a function that is tested monthly, that produces a human-readable document that matches the database state, and that output is retained for a rolling ninety days. If the system goes away tomorrow, she wants to be able to hand an inspector a printed document that stands on its own.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in compliance defensibility and audit posture, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Digital Records vs. Paper Backup
excerpt_text: She also requires what she calls a **sync closure protocol**: every offline work must have a defined completion step where the mechanic confirms sync status before the task is considered closed. Not the system confirming sync. The mechanic confirming it. Because the mechanic is the one who signed their name, and the mechanic is the one who knows whether the work was done.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in field workflow realities (mobile/offline/hangar constraints), suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > What She Needs Athelon to Do That Current Systems Don't
excerpt_text: She has a list. She has had it in various forms for several years. She shared it in the intake interview without being asked.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > What She Needs Athelon to Do That Current Systems Don't
excerpt_text: **1. Unified discrepancy-to-task-card record.** A finding opened during a task card step must be part of that task card's record, not a parallel record in a separate module. Closure of the finding must be a prerequisite for task card completion. The relationship must be architectural, not procedural — she doesn't want to write a shop policy that says "always link your discrepancies." She wants the system to make it impossible not to.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in product capability maturity (current state), suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > What She Needs Athelon to Do That Current Systems Don't
excerpt_text: **2. Inspector qualification check at work order assignment, not after.** When a mechanic is assigned to a task that requires a specific authorization — type-specific training, a limited rating, a non-routine inspection authority — the system must check their current qualification before the assignment confirms. Not after. Before. And if the qualification is within sixty days of expiry, she wants a flag that shows up for her, not just for the mechanic.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > What She Needs Athelon to Do That Current Systems Don't
excerpt_text: **3. Return-to-service readiness gate.** The return-to-service function must refuse to activate if there are open discrepancies, unsigned task cards, or parts-receipt gaps against the work order. Not a warning. A hard stop. She has seen too many close calls where RTS was initiated on a work order that showed green in the main view because the mechanic had closed all the primary tasks — but an open discrepancy, filed as a sub-item, hadn't been touched. The main view was green. The aircraft wasn't ready. And critically: the DOM must not be able to override this gate without a QCM co-authorization. If the gate can be bypassed by someone above her in the production hierarchy, the gate is not a gate.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in risk landscape and failure modes and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > What She Needs Athelon to Do That Current Systems Don't
excerpt_text: **4. Audit trail that doesn't require her to build it.** When she prepares for a surveillance inspection, she currently spends two to three days pulling records, cross-referencing modules, and assembling documentation packages. She wants the system to maintain that package continuously — a work-order-level audit trail that captures every state change, every signature, every linkage, with timestamps, and that can be printed or exported as a complete, self-contained record at any moment. Not on request. Continuously. The package exists as soon as the work order is opened.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in compliance defensibility and audit posture, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > What She Needs Athelon to Do That Current Systems Don't
excerpt_text: **5. Training record expiry that surfaces automatically and correctly.** Not a static report she pulls weekly. An active alert — in the QCM dashboard, in the mechanic's own view, and in the work assignment flow — when a qualification is within the defined window of expiry. And when a qualification lapses, the system must prevent that mechanic from being assigned tasks requiring that qualification. Passively. Without her having to configure a workaround or keep a parallel spreadsheet.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in product capability maturity (current state) and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > What She Needs Athelon to Do That Current Systems Don't
excerpt_text: **6. Internal audit support built into the workflow.** Not a report generator. A way to flag patterns across work orders — the same corrective action narrative appearing repeatedly, the same mechanic having repeated discrepancy opens on a specific task type, completion time anomalies that suggest rubber-stamping. She currently identifies these patterns by reading work orders manually. She wants the system to surface them. She is not asking for AI. She is asking for a QCM-accessible view of work order data that isn't locked behind an admin portal or a vendor support request.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Current Workarounds at TriState
excerpt_text: She keeps a list of things she does manually because the system doesn't do them. She did not volunteer this list in the intake meeting — she mentioned it in passing while explaining her training record concerns, and the interviewer asked to see it. She emailed it that evening. It has eleven items. The relevant ones:
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for risk landscape and failure modes and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Current Workarounds at TriState
excerpt_text: A weekly Excel spreadsheet of all active mechanics, their current qualifications, the aircraft types those qualifications cover, and the expiry date of each. She updates it manually every Monday morning by cross-referencing Ramco with the training provider portals. This takes about ninety minutes.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for risk landscape and failure modes and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Current Workarounds at TriState
excerpt_text: A paper binder she keeps at her desk containing printed, signed copies of the last twelve completed RTS packages for each active aircraft type. She prints these after every return-to-service, signs the print, and files it. She has never needed to use them in an inspection. She keeps them because she does not trust that the Ramco export will work correctly under pressure.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in adoption signals and trust transfer dynamics, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Current Workarounds at TriState

She's asking whether the engineers understand the compliance context behind discrepancy closure, or whether they've modeled it as a checkbox field. She will know which one it is by the answer.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Personality & Interview Notes
excerpt_text: She prints things. Not because she doesn't trust digital records in principle — she just described a sophisticated position on digital record integrity — but because she reviews printed work orders the night before an inspection the same way a lawyer reviews printed briefs. The physical act of reading a printed page is how she catches what the screen smooths over. She does not apologize for this.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Personality & Interview Notes
excerpt_text: She is forty-seven years old, has been in aviation since she was fifteen, has held an IA for fourteen years, and has been running quality systems for nine. She has the kind of institutional memory that gets built by being in the same industry long enough to see the same mistake get made twice. When she references the AVMRO Pro incident or the 2022 SDR records review, she is not venting. She is teaching. She pays attention to whether the person she's teaching is actually learning.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > Personality & Interview Notes

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > One Thing She Will Watch For
excerpt_text: She will be watching, during the embed, for the moment when someone on the Athelon engineering team — well-intentioned, smart, familiar with aviation at the conceptual level — describes a compliance concern as a UX problem. This is not a condescending prediction. She expects it because she has seen it before. The tendency to resolve regulatory complexity by making an interface feel easier is real, and it produces systems that are pleasant to use and legally precarious.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in compliance defensibility and audit posture that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > One Thing She Will Watch For
excerpt_text: The specific version she is braced for: someone will suggest that the return-to-service gate should be a warning instead of a hard stop because users find hard stops frustrating. She will have a response ready. She will be polite about it. She will not change her position.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in operational/governance discipline and controls and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > One Thing She Will Watch For
excerpt_text: She is also watching for the opposite failure: engineers who are so deferential to compliance requirements that they build a system no mechanic will use correctly under time pressure. She has seen those systems too. A sign-off flow that takes four screens and three confirmations will be worked around. It will be worked around officially — by the shop writing a procedure that preauthorizes batched sign-offs — or it will be worked around unofficially, which is worse. She wants a system that is genuinely easy to use correctly and genuinely hard to use incorrectly. She does not think these are in tension. She thinks most systems just haven't tried.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in compliance defensibility and audit posture that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Renata Solís — Quality Control Manager > One Thing She Will Watch For

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Location
excerpt_text: Desert Raptor Aviation — Sheet Metal Shop, Henderson, NV
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Format
excerpt_text: Semi-structured. Felix had a printed maintenance record from a previous job on the table — he'd brought it as a reference point. He referred to it four times.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: Tell me what good structural repair documentation looks like. Starting from scratch.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Felix
excerpt_text: [Sets the printed record aside and pulls out his own.] This is a repair I did on a King Air 200 last year. Skin damage on the aft fuselage — station 469 to station 481, just aft of the pressure vessel. The damage was a dent, roughly 3.75 inches long by 1.1 inches wide at its widest point, 0.082 inches deep at the deepest. I measured it with a depth micrometer, not estimated it.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: And the record you brought in for comparison?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Felix
excerpt_text: [Slides the highlighted page across.] This came in with an aircraft we received for an annual inspection. Previous shop did a fuselage skin repair. This is the maintenance record. Read it.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Felix
excerpt_text: That's all of it. No location. No dimensions. No SRM chapter. No figure number. No description of the repair methodology. No fastener specification. No material specification. I don't know where on the fuselage this repair is. I don't know how large the damage was. I don't know if what they did is actually within the SRM's authorization. I don't know if a doubler was installed or if they blended the damage. I know nothing.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Felix
excerpt_text: [Pause.] Marginally. At minimum. Part 43.9 requires a description of work performed. This says "repaired damaged skin." It's a description in the loosest possible sense. An FAA inspector with high standards would write them up. An inspector on a good day might not. But even if it passes a compliance check, it fails as a maintenance record. The purpose of the record is to document what happened. This doesn't document what happened.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: Does software contribute to records like this being written?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Felix
excerpt_text: Directly. If you give a mechanic a blank text field and ask them to describe the work, they will write as much as they need to write to satisfy the minimum they believe is required. Some techs have high minimums. Most don't. A software system that provides structured fields — damage location, damage type, damage dimensions, SRM reference with chapter/section/subject broken out — prompts the tech to provide the complete information. Not because they want to, necessarily. Because the field is there and empty and the system won't let them move forward without filling it.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in future-state roadmap and expansion vectors, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: Describe the structural repair record structure you'd design for this software.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Felix
excerpt_text: Two separate sections, minimum. Damage section and repair section.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Damage section
excerpt_text: Aircraft station and buttline or waterline reference, or fuselage area description for aircraft without published station data. Skin zone — upper, lower, side, which side. Structure type — fuselage skin, rib, stringer, spar, frame. Damage type — enum field, not free text: dent, crack, corrosion (and sub-type: pitting, intergranular, exfoliation), abrasion, puncture, disbond for composites. Dimensions — length, width, depth, all numeric. Proximity — distance to nearest fastener, nearest seam, nearest structural member, all numeric. Photos attached — mandatory field, not optional.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Repair section
excerpt_text: Disposition type — enum: within-limits no repair, repair per SRM, repair per EO/DER, major repair with 337. SRM reference — structured: manual title, report number, revision level, chapter number and title, section number, subject number, and figure/table number. Separately for each element. Not one text field. Each element its own field. That makes them searchable.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in risk landscape and failure modes that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: That's significantly more structure than any current MRO software I've seen.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Felix
excerpt_text: Because most MRO software was designed for oil changes and filter replacements. A filter replacement record is simple. An oil and filter: part number, quantity, work performed, date, signature. That's a text field situation. A structural repair record is not a filter replacement record. The software shouldn't treat them the same.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for data integrity and traceability architecture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: Let's talk about DER coordination. Walk me through how you work with Bob Hwang on a repair that needs his sign-off.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Felix
excerpt_text: Damage comes in that's beyond SRM limits, or in primary structure where I'm not comfortable with a field repair without engineering review. I write up my findings — sketch, measurements, photos. I send it to Bob via email. He asks questions. I answer. Sometimes he asks me to take more measurements. We go back and forth — could be two emails, could be eight. Eventually he issues me a disposition: either "approved per the following method" with a specific repair design, or "major repair, prepare a 337." That disposition has a number. Bob tracks it in his own records.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for operational/governance discipline and controls and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: And the Form 337 — you mentioned eleven of these in your career. What's the worst part of the current process?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Felix
excerpt_text: The disconnection. I prepare the form. I submit it. Oklahoma City receives it. The form becomes part of the aircraft's permanent record in FAA's database. But in my shop's software, the work order that generated the 337 has no reference to that form number. And if I'm looking at the aircraft three years later and I see a repair and I want to know if a 337 was filed for it, I'm searching by memory or by physical file. I want a field on the work order: "FAA Form 337 filed — form number — date submitted — submission method." Two fields. That's it. Then I can pull up any work order and immediately know whether a 337 was involved.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Felix
excerpt_text: SRM reference completeness is always the first thing they check if they're looking at a structural repair. They want to see the chapter and section, not just "per SRM." Second is the damage description — can they identify where on the aircraft this damage was, independent of the record author? Third is the repair disposition — was a 337 required and if so, was it filed? Fourth — and this is newer, last three or four years — photo documentation. Inspectors have started expecting to see photos attached to structural damage findings. If there's no photo, they ask why.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: What would it take for you to trust the maintenance record that Athelon generates? Not write — generates.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for adoption signals and trust transfer dynamics and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Felix
excerpt_text: I want to see the rendered record before I sign it. The whole thing, in its final format. Not the form I filled out — the document that would be shown to an inspector. Every field I entered, displayed as it will appear in the exported record. If there's a formatting issue — if my SRM reference is truncated, if my damage dimensions lost a decimal place — I need to see it before I sign. After I sign, the record is what it is. I need to sign something that looks right.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: Last thing. You mentioned photos are becoming expected. How is photo documentation working or not working in your current process?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Felix
excerpt_text: I take photos with my personal phone. I email them to the shop's shared inbox. Someone — usually the office assistant — downloads them and uploads them to the EBIS 5 work order. That process takes between two hours and two days depending on how busy the office is. I have had an inspector arrive for a review visit when the photos from a job I completed three days earlier were not yet uploaded to the work order. That conversation was uncomfortable.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Marcus
excerpt_text: Felix — and this is a personal question as much as a regulatory one — what's the consequence of a structural repair record that's incomplete? The scenario where the record says "repaired per SRM" and that's all.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Felix
excerpt_text: [Long pause.] Best case: an inspector flags it, writes up a paperwork violation, the shop pays a fine, the record gets amended. That's the best case.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
speaker: Felix
excerpt_text: Give me structured fields and I'll give you complete records. That's the deal.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

The purpose of the record is to document what happened. This doesn't document what happened.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: **Felix:** Directly. If you give a mechanic a blank text field and ask them to describe the work, they will write as much as they need to write to satisfy the minimum they believe is required. Some techs have high minimums. Most don't. A software system that provides structured fields — damage location, damage type, damage dimensions, SRM reference with chapter/section/subject broken out — prompts the tech to provide the complete information. Not because they want to, necessarily. Because the field is there and empty and the system won't let them move forward without filling it.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in future-state roadmap and expansion vectors, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: **Damage section:** Aircraft station and buttline or waterline reference, or fuselage area description for aircraft without published station data. Skin zone — upper, lower, side, which side. Structure type — fuselage skin, rib, stringer, spar, frame. Damage type — enum field, not free text: dent, crack, corrosion (and sub-type: pitting, intergranular, exfoliation), abrasion, puncture, disbond for composites. Dimensions — length, width, depth, all numeric. Proximity — distance to nearest fastener, nearest seam, nearest structural member, all numeric. Photos attached — mandatory field, not optional.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: **Repair section:** Disposition type — enum: within-limits no repair, repair per SRM, repair per EO/DER, major repair with 337. SRM reference — structured: manual title, report number, revision level, chapter number and title, section number, subject number, and figure/table number. Separately for each element. Not one text field. Each element its own field. That makes them searchable.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in risk landscape and failure modes that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: If the disposition is EO or DER: Engineering Order number, DER name, DER FAA authorization number, date of DER concurrence. If the disposition involves a 337: Form 337 reference number, submission date, submission method, Aircraft Registry confirmation number if received.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: **Felix:** Because most MRO software was designed for oil changes and filter replacements. A filter replacement record is simple. An oil and filter: part number, quantity, work performed, date, signature. That's a text field situation. A structural repair record is not a filter replacement record. The software shouldn't treat them the same.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for data integrity and traceability architecture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: Give me different record types. A "structural repair" work type that presents the structured form I described. A "routine maintenance" work type that stays simple. The tech selects the type when they create the task card, and the maintenance record form adjusts accordingly. That's not complicated to build. It's just a choice about whether the software respects the complexity of the work.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in adoption signals and trust transfer dynamics, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: **Nadia:** Let's talk about DER coordination. Walk me through how you work with Bob Hwang on a repair that needs his sign-off.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: **Felix:** Damage comes in that's beyond SRM limits, or in primary structure where I'm not comfortable with a field repair without engineering review. I write up my findings — sketch, measurements, photos. I send it to Bob via email. He asks questions. I answer. Sometimes he asks me to take more measurements. We go back and forth — could be two emails, could be eight. Eventually he issues me a disposition: either "approved per the following method" with a specific repair design, or "major repair, prepare a 337." That disposition has a number. Bob tracks it in his own records.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for operational/governance discipline and controls and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: That whole exchange — the emails, the sketches, the final disposition — is the engineering record for the repair. Right now it lives in my email inbox and in a paper file in the back room. I attach a summary to the work order in EBIS 5, but the summary is my summary. The actual exchange is elsewhere.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: What I want: the ability to create an "external engineering consultation" record in the software. I upload the disposition document — the EO or the approval letter or whatever Bob sends me — and I enter the structured fields: DER name, FAA authorization number, disposition number, date of approval, summary of approved method. That becomes part of the work order record, not a separate paper file. And when I do the return-to-service, the DER reference shows up as a referenced document in the maintenance record, not just as a text note.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: **Nadia:** And the Form 337 — you mentioned eleven of these in your career. What's the worst part of the current process?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: **Felix:** The disconnection. I prepare the form. I submit it. Oklahoma City receives it. The form becomes part of the aircraft's permanent record in FAA's database. But in my shop's software, the work order that generated the 337 has no reference to that form number. And if I'm looking at the aircraft three years later and I see a repair and I want to know if a 337 was filed for it, I'm searching by memory or by physical file. I want a field on the work order: "FAA Form 337 filed — form number — date submitted — submission method." Two fields. That's it. Then I can pull up any work order and immediately know whether a 337 was involved.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: I wish the software would treat the Form 337 as a related document type — like a linked record — so you can navigate from the work order to the 337 reference and back. Not that the software generates the form, necessarily. The FAA has their own system for that. But I want the linkage to exist in my maintenance records, not just on paper.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: **Felix:** SRM reference completeness is always the first thing they check if they're looking at a structural repair. They want to see the chapter and section, not just "per SRM." Second is the damage description — can they identify where on the aircraft this damage was, independent of the record author? Third is the repair disposition — was a 337 required and if so, was it filed? Fourth — and this is newer, last three or four years — photo documentation. Inspectors have started expecting to see photos attached to structural damage findings. If there's no photo, they ask why.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: In all three surveillance audits I've been through at Desert Raptor, my records have been the longest in the work order stack. The inspector always reads them first and then moves faster through the rest because he trusts the shop's record quality after reading mine. That's not me being arrogant. That's me saying: complete records build trust faster than short records, even with inspectors who know the regs cold.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in compliance defensibility and audit posture, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: **Felix:** I want to see the rendered record before I sign it. The whole thing, in its final format. Not the form I filled out — the document that would be shown to an inspector. Every field I entered, displayed as it will appear in the exported record. If there's a formatting issue — if my SRM reference is truncated, if my damage dimensions lost a decimal place — I need to see it before I sign. After I sign, the record is what it is. I need to sign something that looks right.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: And the SRM reference needs to render as a complete citation, not as a database dump. If I enter chapter 53, section 41, subject 10, figure 53-41-10-2 in four separate fields, the rendered record should show that as a coherent, readable citation: "Ref: [Manual Title], Rev A12, Chapter 53, Section 41, Subject 10, Fig. 53-41-10-2." Not four separate database rows.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in risk landscape and failure modes and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: I wish the software would render structured fields into readable prose for the final record, while keeping the structured data searchable in the background. Those two things are compatible. You can store structure and display prose. Most aviation software does neither — it stores unstructured text and displays it as-is. That's not good enough.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: **Felix:** I take photos with my personal phone. I email them to the shop's shared inbox. Someone — usually the office assistant — downloads them and uploads them to the EBIS 5 work order. That process takes between two hours and two days depending on how busy the office is. I have had an inspector arrive for a review visit when the photos from a job I completed three days earlier were not yet uploaded to the work order. That conversation was uncomfortable.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: What should happen: I take the photo at the aircraft. The photo goes directly into the work order record, tagged with GPS coordinates and timestamp, attached to the specific damage finding I'm documenting. From phone to record in one step. Not phone to email to inbox to download to upload to link.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in market/backstory and mission origin, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: I wish the software would let me photograph a finding in the app and attach it directly to the damage record I'm creating, with the timestamp and my user identity automatically embedded in the attachment metadata. That's not a future feature. That's what every other industry figured out in 2016.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: **Marcus:** Felix — and this is a personal question as much as a regulatory one — what's the consequence of a structural repair record that's incomplete? The scenario where the record says "repaired per SRM" and that's all.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: **Felix:** [Long pause.] Best case: an inspector flags it, writes up a paperwork violation, the shop pays a fine, the record gets amended. That's the best case.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: Middle case: the aircraft changes hands. The new owner's shop does maintenance. They find the repair — the doubler, the rivet pattern, whatever was installed. They have no documentation to verify the repair meets structural requirements. They have to get a DER involved to assess the repair independently, which costs time and money. The aircraft may be grounded during the assessment.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for operational/governance discipline and controls and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: Worst case: the repair was not adequate. Not within SRM limits, not per an approved method. Nobody knew because the record didn't say what was done. Years later, under fatigue loading, the structure fails. And the record — the record I'm showing you — is the only paper trail that exists for why that structure was the way it was.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview — Felix Okafor, Structural Repair Technician
excerpt_text: That's the consequence. That's why I write three-page maintenance records for a dent that other techs document in one sentence.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.
