section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Background
excerpt_text: Felix Okafor was born in Lagos and came to the US with his family at age nine. He grew up in South Phoenix, discovered EAA Chapter 724 at age seventeen (a neighbor was restoring a Stinson 108), and had his A&P certificate three months after his twenty-first birthday. He spent four years at a sheet metal shop in Phoenix doing mostly Bonanza and Baron structural work before moving to Desert Raptor, where the presence of King Air and light jet traffic gave him more complex structural work than he'd seen before.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Background
excerpt_text: He is a careful, thorough writer of maintenance records — a quality that is rarer in his specialty than it should be. He believes the maintenance record for a structural repair is a legal document that needs to tell the complete story: what damage was found, how it was measured, what reference was used to determine if it was within limits or required repair, what repair method was applied, and what the final condition of the repaired structure is. He does not accept shorthand. He has marked up another tech's work order three times in the last two years because the damage description was inadequate.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in market/backstory and mission origin, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Background
excerpt_text: He also has specific aesthetic frustrations: he finds the physical format of most maintenance record text fields — narrow, word-wrap-heavy, no line breaks in the rendered output — makes his carefully structured records unreadable. He has started writing his structural repair narratives in a Word document first, then copy-pasting into EBIS 5. He loses the formatting every time. This infuriates him in a quiet, professional way.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Work Pattern
excerpt_text: **Type 1 — In-Limits Damage (SRM reference):** Aircraft arrives with a dent, scratch, or corrosion finding. Felix inspects, measures, references the applicable SRM, determines the damage is within allowable limits per the chart (or isn't, and proceeds to repair). Logs the finding and the disposition — in-limits or repair required — with the SRM chapter/section reference. This is the most common case.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Work Pattern
excerpt_text: **Type 2 — Repair Per SRM:** Damage exceeds in-limits but the repair is covered by the SRM. Felix designs the repair using the SRM methodology — rivet pattern, doubler sizing, material specification. Logs the repair with the complete SRM reference: manual title, revision level, chapter, section, subject, and figure number.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in risk landscape and failure modes that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Work Pattern
excerpt_text: **Type 3 — Engineering Order (EO) or DER Approval:** Damage exceeds what the SRM covers, or the repair location is in a primary structure area requiring specific engineering sign-off. Felix writes up the damage, contacts Bob Hwang (DER), provides measurements and photos. Bob issues an Engineering Order or approves a repair design. Felix performs the repair, logs it with the EO or DER approval number, and Bob co-signs the return-to-service documentation.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Work Pattern
excerpt_text: **Type 4 — Major Repair (FAA Form 337):** A subset of Type 3 where the repair constitutes a major repair under Part 43, Appendix A, and requires FAA Form 337 to be submitted to the Oklahoma City Aircraft Registry. Felix has completed 11 Form 337s in his career. He knows the form. He is very specific about what goes in Box 8 (description of work accomplished).
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Specific Pain Points with Current Software (EBIS 5) > 1. SRM References Are Freeform Text — No Structure
excerpt_text: The most important piece of information in a structural repair record is the SRM reference: Manual title, revision level, chapter, section, subject (CSS format). Example:
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Specific Pain Points with Current Software (EBIS 5) > 1. SRM References Are Freeform Text — No Structure
excerpt_text: > *Cessna 172 Structural Repair Manual, Revision 4, Chapter 53 — Fuselage, Section 10 — Fuselage Structure, Subject 10 — Allowable Damage — Table 53-10-10-1.*
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Specific Pain Points with Current Software (EBIS 5) > 1. SRM References Are Freeform Text — No Structure
excerpt_text: In EBIS 5, this goes in a text field. There is no structure. A new tech logs it as "Cessna SRM Ch 53." An experienced tech logs the full reference. An inspector comparing the two records gets wildly different information. There's no standardization, no enforcement, and no way to search by SRM chapter across work orders.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Specific Pain Points with Current Software (EBIS 5) > 2. Damage Description Has No Standardized Format
excerpt_text: Felix's internal standard for damage description is: *Location (station/stringer/rib reference), Type (dent/crack/corrosion/abrasion), Dimensions (length × width × depth), Extent (isolated/multiple areas), Proximity to structure (distance to nearest fastener/seam/edge).* EBIS 5 offers one text field. The damage description field and the repair description field are the same field. Felix separates them with a blank line and a header. In the rendered record, it's a wall of text.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Specific Pain Points with Current Software (EBIS 5) > 3. DER Reference Has No Dedicated Field
excerpt_text: When Bob Hwang issues an EO or provides a one-time engineering disposition on a repair, that document has a number. The number — and the DER's FAA authorization number — should be in the maintenance record. EBIS 5 has no field for DER reference numbers, Engineering Order numbers, or DER identification numbers. Felix writes them in the "work performed" field. The information is there if you read carefully. It's not retrievable by search.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Specific Pain Points with Current Software (EBIS 5) > 4. FAA Form 337 Is Disconnected from the Work Order
excerpt_text: When a major repair requires a Form 337, the form is submitted to Oklahoma City via mail (or increasingly, electronic submission). Desert Raptor keeps a copy. The work order that generated the 337 should reference the 337 number, and the 337 should reference the work order number. In EBIS 5, there's no field to link the two. Felix writes "See attached 337 — WO#XXXXX" on the paper 337 and "337 submitted — see file" in the work order. The linkage exists on paper. It does not exist in the software.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Specific Pain Points with Current Software (EBIS 5) > 5. Repair Records Don't Describe the Repair Methodology Adequately
excerpt_text: This is Felix's largest frustration, and the one he cares most deeply about. A structural repair record, in his view, must answer: what method was used? What materials? What fasteners? What rivet pattern? What doubler dimensions? What final edge distances? These are the elements that would allow another engineer — or an inspector, or a future mechanic at a different shop — to verify the repair is correct and repeatable.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Specific Pain Points with Current Software (EBIS 5) > 5. Repair Records Don't Describe the Repair Methodology Adequately
excerpt_text: He's seen repair records from other shops — both at Desert Raptor on incoming aircraft and at previous jobs — that say "repaired per SRM" with no further detail. Under Part 43.9, a maintenance record must contain a description of the work performed. "Repaired per SRM" is not a description. It is a reference without content. Felix considers it non-compliant. He makes his records longer than anyone else's in the shop. He doesn't apologize for it.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Opinions on Aviation Software
excerpt_text: **On repair record format:** > "A structural repair record should read like a technical document, not a sticky note. If another engineer has to certify this repair twenty years from now because the aircraft is going through a major overhaul, they need to be able to read my record and know exactly what I did. 'Repaired per SRM' gives them nothing. Give me a format. Give me sections. Give me fields that prompt for the right information."
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in market/backstory and mission origin, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Opinions on Aviation Software
excerpt_text: **On DER relationships and documentation:** > "Working with a DER is a collaborative process. Bob and I go back and forth on repair designs sometimes. That back-and-forth — the measurements I sent, the approval he gave, the specific conditions he attached to his disposition — is part of the record. Right now it lives in my email. Email is not a maintenance record. The software should give me a way to attach that history to the work order."
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in operational/governance discipline and controls, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Opinions on Aviation Software
excerpt_text: **On the 337 process:** > "The 337 is a federal document that becomes part of the aircraft's permanent record. If my work order software doesn't know a 337 exists for a job, and someone three years later is trying to piece together why the fuselage has a doubler in that location, they're going to have to call Oklahoma City. I should be able to pull up the work order and see: 337 submitted, date, form number. Two seconds, not a phone call."
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in market/backstory and mission origin, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Technician Profile — Felix Okafor > Opinions on Aviation Software
excerpt_text: **On the value of searchable records:** > "I want to be able to search my previous work orders by SRM chapter. If I've done a fuselage skin repair before, I want to find every previous job where I referenced Chapter 53 of that SRM. Not because I don't remember how to do it — because the previous job might have details or measurements I can compare. Right now I search by memory. That's fine when you have a good memory. It doesn't scale."
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

excerpt_text: Felix represents the highest-documentation-quality technician profile we've encountered in the simulation. His requirements will push the maintenance record schema and UI to be more structured than currently specified.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for data integrity and traceability architecture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > A Representative Major Repair — What the Workflow Should Look Like
speaker: Scenario
excerpt_text: A King Air B200 arrives with impact damage to the aft fuselage skin — a panel that was struck by ground support equipment. The damage is approximately 4.5 inches in the long axis, with a small crack propagating from the lower edge of the dent toward the aft pressure bulkhead frame. The crack changes the classification: this is not a dent-within-limits situation.

Everything that matters about the repair — materials, fastener specifications, edge distances, final condition — goes in the maintenance record. Not because the FAA is coming. Because the aircraft is going to be maintained by someone else in five years.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > Background
excerpt_text: Renata Vasquez grew up in Corpus Christi, three miles from the airport, and spent her adolescence watching the Navy T-34s and T-44s fly patterns at NAS Corpus Christi before the flight training mission moved out. Her uncle worked line operations at the old Naval Air Depot; she didn't understand exactly what he did, but she understood that aviation maintenance was work you could be serious about. She enrolled in A&P school at Del Mar College at 19 and had her certificate at 21.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > Background
excerpt_text: Her first job was at a small FBO doing Cessna 172 and Piper Cherokee maintenance — the standard proving ground. Within two years she was being handed the structural write-ups because she was methodical and she wasn't afraid of the SRM. At 25, she was hired by a heavy MRO operator in San Antonio running 737 line maintenance and heavy checks, where she spent nine years in the structural shop doing skin repairs, corrosion mapping, and major inspection structural work on 737 Classics and early NGs. It was there that she submitted her first Form 337, then her twentieth, then her hundredth. The 737 world generates a steady stream of major structural repairs — fuselage skin panels, stringer repairs, frame repairs — and the Form 337 paperwork chain is not optional. She knows the form the way a tax accountant knows a 1040.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for data integrity and traceability architecture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > Background
excerpt_text: She also learned, in those nine years, that the documentation chain around a major structural repair is not a bureaucratic formality. It is the record that tells the next engineer — who might be looking at this airframe in three years, or fifteen, or thirty — what happened here, why, and what was done about it. She has pulled aircraft records on incoming King Airs at Gulf Coast and found major structural repairs with no Form 337 in the aircraft's permanent file. Every time it happens, she files a discrepancy report and contacts the prior operator. Every time, the story is the same: the 337 was done on paper, the paper got separated from the aircraft logbook during a sale, and now nobody can reconstruct the approval chain for a structural modification that is still on the airplane. She is not the kind of person who accepts that as a normal outcome.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for future-state roadmap and expansion vectors and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > Background
excerpt_text: She moved back to Corpus Christi in 2017 when Gulf Coast Structural offered her a lead structural tech position. The shop does King Air and Citation structural work, some 737 field repair contract work, and occasional calls on 767 structurals when a regional airline needs a specialist on-site. She has her IA and uses it regularly — primarily for structural RTS sign-offs on major repairs and for annuals on owner-flown King Airs.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for operational/governance discipline and controls and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > Background
excerpt_text: She is the person the DOM calls when a structural write-up needs to hold up to FAA scrutiny. She has been through four Certificate Management Office audits at two different shops. Her records have never been a finding. She attributes this entirely to the fact that she treats every Form 337 and every maintenance record as if an FSDO investigator will read it next week. They usually won't. Sometimes they will. She does not adjust her behavior based on the probability of being audited.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > Background
excerpt_text: She agreed to the Athelon embed when the DOM forwarded the opportunity notice. She had one question before she said yes: does Athelon have a Form 337 reference field in the major repair workflow? She was told it had been added in v2.1. She said she'd come take a look.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > How She Actually Does Structural Work
excerpt_text: Renata's workflow is built around a chain of decisions, each of which has a documentation trigger. She thinks of the work order as the spine of that chain — everything else hangs off it. The sequence matters and she doesn't compress it.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > How She Actually Does Structural Work
excerpt_text: **Step 1 — Damage characterization.** Before she touches the aircraft, she opens a work order and enters the damage description in the format she's been using for twelve years: location (station, stringer, frame, rib, or equivalent per structural drawing), type (crack, corrosion, dent, abrasion, impact, disbond), dimensions (length × width × depth), and proximity to primary structure (distance to nearest fastener, seam, or edge). This is not optional documentation she'll get to later. If she opens a work order and the damage description says "skin damage aft fuselage," she writes her own before she opens a panel. She has refused to start work on jobs where she couldn't establish a documented damage baseline.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > How She Actually Does Structural Work
excerpt_text: **Step 2 — SRM disposition.** Is the damage within allowable limits per the applicable SRM, or does it require repair? If repair, is it covered by the SRM? Every structural decision traces to a specific manual, a specific revision, a specific chapter, section, subject, and figure. Not "the SRM." The specific revision of the SRM that applies to this serial number, accounting for any Supplemental Type Certificates that alter structural allowables. She has argued with DERs who cited the wrong SRM revision. She was right both times. One of those arguments involved a King Air fuselage stringer that was borderline on the allowable damage chart — the difference between the old revision and the current one was whether it was in-limits or required a repair. The revision matters.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in data integrity and traceability architecture and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > How She Actually Does Structural Work
excerpt_text: **Step 3 — Repair classification.** Is this a minor repair or a major repair under 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix A? This is not a judgment call she makes loosely. The criteria are specific: primary structural members, repairs that affect the structural strength or flight characteristics of the aircraft, repairs to pressurized structure, repairs involving engineering design data not provided in the Type Certificate. She has encountered shops where the classification defaulted to "minor" unless someone specifically escalated it. At her shops, the classification is made affirmatively — she documents why something is minor as often as she documents why it's major.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > How She Actually Does Structural Work
excerpt_text: **Step 4 — The 337 chain.** When a major repair classification is established, the work order is immediately flagged. The Form 337 is prepared in parallel with the repair, not after. She does not start a major repair without the 337 framework in place because she has seen what happens when you do: the repair gets done, the aircraft sits on the ramp waiting to go, the customer calls, and someone decides the paperwork can be filed retroactively. It usually gets filed. Sometimes it doesn't. When it doesn't, the aircraft flies with an unrecorded major repair until someone notices. She doesn't work in shops that tolerate this, and she doesn't contribute to it herself.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for data integrity and traceability architecture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > How She Actually Does Structural Work
excerpt_text: **Step 5 — DER involvement, when required.** Repairs that exceed SRM coverage, or that involve primary structure without approved repair data, go to a DER. Alejandro Reyes is her primary — they've worked together long enough that she can send him a damage photo and a dimensional sketch and he'll tell her within an hour whether he can write a disposition or whether it needs a full engineering analysis. When Alejandro signs off a repair, his FAA authorization number, the document reference, and any approval conditions go into the maintenance record. This is not optional.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > How She Actually Does Structural Work
excerpt_text: **Step 6 — RTS.** She doesn't sign the return to service until every prior step is documented and she can see the complete chain. Aircraft N-number and serial number. Work order number. Damage description. SRM reference. Repair classification. Form 337 reference number and the name of who prepared the 337. DER reference if applicable. Everything on one screen. If anything is missing, she asks for it. She has held aircraft for two days waiting for a 337 to be properly filed. The customer was unhappy. The aircraft was correctly documented.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in data integrity and traceability architecture that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > Paper vs. Software History
excerpt_text: Renata has used five distinct MRO systems over her career, ranging from a functioning regional standard to a product that will permanently affect how she evaluates software.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for data integrity and traceability architecture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > Paper vs. Software History
excerpt_text: **System 1 — CAMP Systems (early career FBO, ~2003–2005):** Used primarily for scheduled maintenance tracking. She was a junior tech interacting with it only tangentially — squawks, task sign-offs, parts requests. No structural documentation workflow relevant at that career stage. No complaints.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > Paper vs. Software History
excerpt_text: **System 2 — MRO Pro (San Antonio heavy MRO, 2004–2013):** Her primary system for nine years. MRO Pro had a "repair type" dropdown with values including "major repair" and "minor repair." This was encouraging. What it did not have was any enforcement downstream of that selection. Selecting "major repair" changed nothing about the work order workflow. There was no Form 337 reference field anywhere in the system. The DOM at that shop solved this by requiring a paper Form 337 copy to be physically attached to every major repair work order jacket before the job could be billed out. The folder left the shop. The software record said nothing. If you searched the MRO Pro database for all major structural repairs on a given tail number, you found work orders with no indication of whether a 337 had been filed. The information lived in physical folders in filing cabinets in the DOM's office, organized by aircraft N-number. For the nine years she was there, this worked because the DOM was meticulous. When the DOM left the company in 2012, it stopped working. The filing system degraded. She watched it happen in her last year there and it was one of the reasons she left.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > Paper vs. Software History
excerpt_text: **System 3 — AvMaint Direct (San Antonio, 2012–2013):** The shop trialed AvMaint Direct for eight months before abandoning it. AvMaint Direct had no repair type classification at all on work orders. "Major repair" and "minor repair" were not concepts the software recognized. There was no Form 337 field. There was no DER reference field. There was no way to link any regulatory document to a work order. She described the experience in her intake interview without emotion, in the flat tone she uses when something has already been processed and filed: *"It was designed by someone who had never encountered an FAA Form 337 and possibly didn't know they existed. I don't mean that to be harsh. I mean that they literally did not account for the regulatory structure that governs what we do. For structural work, it was useless. For anything requiring compliance documentation, it was worse than paper because paper at least has room to write."* She spent those eight months keeping a parallel paper log of all her structural work. When the shop abandoned AvMaint Direct, she was not surprised. She also understood that those eight months of work orders existed in a system with no 337 tracking and no classification records. The repairs were documented — she documented them herself, on paper — but the software record of that period was structurally incomplete in a way that could not be reconstructed.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in compliance defensibility and audit posture, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > Paper vs. Software History
excerpt_text: **System 4 — Corridor (San Antonio, 2013):** Used only for the last few months before she returned to Corpus Christi. Corridor had meaningfully better structure than MRO Pro: "major repair" classification triggered a DOM review flag, which was genuine progress. There was still no dedicated 337 reference field, but the flag at least ensured the DOM touched every major repair record before it closed. The 337 documentation still lived outside the system, but the human check was enforced. She considers Corridor the first system she used that showed evidence of someone understanding the compliance picture.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > Paper vs. Software History
excerpt_text: **System 5 — WingSpan MRO (Gulf Coast Structural, 2017–present):** The most complete structural documentation workflow she'd encountered before Athelon. WingSpan has a repair type field, an "EO/DER reference" field, and — added in the 2021 update — a field labeled "FAA Form 337 Ref #." Real progress. The problem: the field exists but the system does not block RTS authorization when the field is empty on a major repair work order. It is advisory. A tech can authorize RTS, the system will proceed, and the 337 reference field will remain blank in the permanent record. She has seen this happen — not at Gulf Coast Structural, where the shop culture and her own presence create a floor, but at another WingSpan shop whose aircraft come through for follow-on work. Two aircraft in three years arrived with major repair work orders showing empty 337 fields. Both were compliance findings. Both required retroactive paperwork and FSDO notification. Both were caused entirely by the fact that WingSpan treated the 337 reference as optional at the enforcement point. *"An advisory field on a mandatory document is not a field,"* she says. *"It's a suggestion. We have enough suggestions in aviation. We need more enforcement."*
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in compliance defensibility and audit posture and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > The Form 337 — Her Requirements, In Her Words
excerpt_text: Asked to describe exactly what she needs from any MRO system handling major structural repair documentation, Renata's answer is specific and does not waver:
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > The Form 337 — Her Requirements, In Her Words
excerpt_text: > "When I classify a work order as major repair — or when the system prompts for that classification, which it should do if I select a repair type that falls under Part 43 Appendix A — the system needs to know from that moment forward that this work order does not close without a Form 337 reference. Not a warning. A block. The RTS authorization button should be grayed out. The sign-off screen should say: 'Form 337 Reference Required.' Not in small print. Not as a checkbox I can check without entering a number. There should be a field, and it should require a value, and until that value is entered, nobody signs the RTS."
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in compliance defensibility and audit posture and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > The Form 337 — Her Requirements, In Her Words
excerpt_text: On the question of what happens when the 337 hasn't been submitted yet at the time of RTS — a real scenario in field operations:
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > The Form 337 — Her Requirements, In Her Words
excerpt_text: > "That situation actually happens. The repair is done. The 337 is prepared. In some shops the IA signs the 337 before RTS; in some shops the 337 goes to the DER for co-signature first, which takes time. What I need is the ability to enter a preliminary 337 identifier — our internal tracking number if the FAA document number isn't assigned yet — and have the system record the date and the person who prepared the form. Then when the FAA submission is confirmed, update the field with the final 337 document number. What I cannot have is the RTS signed with the field empty because we're 'waiting for the DER.' That's the moment where shortcuts happen. Something real has to be in that field before RTS. Even if it's our internal tracking number. The field cannot be empty."
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in compliance defensibility and audit posture and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > The Form 337 — Her Requirements, In Her Words
excerpt_text: > "Before I put my IA certificate number on a return to service for a major structural repair, I need to see: aircraft N-number and serial number, work order number, work description, repair classification stated explicitly as major repair, Form 337 reference number, and the name of the person who prepared the 337. All of that on one screen before I sign. I want to be looking at the complete chain. If any element is missing, I'm not signing. The software should make that an impossible situation rather than a choice I have to make."
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in data integrity and traceability architecture and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > Learning That Athelon Has the Field — But No UI Workflow Yet
excerpt_text: Then the follow-up: the UI does not yet have a dedicated workflow for the Form 337 reference field. There is no prompt when a work order is classified as major repair. There is no visual indicator on the work order card that the field is missing or required. The input field is not surfaced in the standard work order edit view. The `form_337_ref` field exists in the schema and the validation exists in the RTS authorization backend, but the path to populate the field currently requires raw work order editing or direct API access.

excerpt_text: > "So the right architecture is there but the interface doesn't surface it, and a tech in the field has no idea the field exists unless someone tells them. Which means the first time they try to authorize RTS on a major repair, it fails with a validation error they don't understand. They either find someone who knows about the field, or they find a workaround. The validation block is real. The problem is that the field should be visible and required from the moment the work order is classified as major repair — not revealed as a surprise when RTS fails at the end of the job."

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > What She Will Be Watching During the Embed
excerpt_text: Renata has a short list. She adds to it when she encounters something she didn't anticipate, but the core items were fixed before she arrived.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for future-state roadmap and expansion vectors and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > What She Will Be Watching During the Embed
excerpt_text: **1. The Form 337 field — when and where it appears in the UI.** Currently it does not appear in the work order edit view for any user role. Target: present from the moment the repair type is set to `major_repair`, marked required, with a status indicator on the work order card (something like "337 Ref: PENDING / ENTERED / N/A").

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > What She Will Be Watching During the Embed
excerpt_text: **2. Whether the RTS block is communicated before the tech attempts to authorize.** She does not want the first indication of the 337 requirement to be a validation error at the RTS screen. The work order should carry a visible status from the moment it's classified as a major repair — open, incomplete, blocked. The RTS authorization should be confirmation, not discovery.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in compliance defensibility and audit posture and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > What She Will Be Watching During the Embed
excerpt_text: **3. The error message quality on RTS validation failure.** When the block fires because `form_337_ref` is null, what does the tech see? A generic validation error is insufficient. The message needs to name the specific field, explain why it's required (major repair classification), and tell the tech where to go to populate it. She will test this deliberately.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > What She Will Be Watching During the Embed
excerpt_text: **4. The DER reference pathway.** Athelon's schema currently has `form_337_ref`. She wants to know whether there is also a path to record the DER's information — DER name, FAA authorization number, repair design document reference — for engineered major repairs that required a DER disposition. If it's all collapsed into the same freeform field, she'll work with it but she'll document why structured subfields would be better.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > What She Will Be Watching During the Embed
excerpt_text: **5. The SRM reference structure.** When a tech records the SRM reference for a structural repair, is it freeform text or does it have structure — manual title, revision level, chapter, section, subject? She expects freeform. She'll push for structure. She knows this is a longer conversation that may not resolve during the embed, but she'll start it.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in risk landscape and failure modes that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > What She Will Be Watching During the Embed
excerpt_text: **6. Tail-number historical view for major repairs.** Can she pull up an aircraft and see all major repair work orders, with their 337 references, sorted by date, without drilling into each work order individually? For a DER reviewing an airframe's structural history before approving a new repair, this view is the starting point. If it doesn't exist, she'll ask for it.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for data integrity and traceability architecture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > What She Will Be Watching During the Embed
excerpt_text: **7. Repair type in reporting.** The DOM at Gulf Coast runs a monthly structural summary. It must segment major repairs from minor repairs because the major repair count feeds into the FAA reporting picture for the Part 145 certificate. If Athelon's reporting layer doesn't filter and group by repair classification, that's a gap for compliance management customers.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > What She Will Be Watching During the Embed
excerpt_text: **8. The printed maintenance record output.** What the work order looks like when it's exported or printed as a maintenance record is the document that follows the aircraft. It needs to include the repair classification and the 337 reference number. She will generate a test major repair work order, complete it including the 337 reference, and examine the exported record. If the 337 reference is not on the output, it has to be added.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > A Representative Major Repair — What the Workflow Should Look Like
excerpt_text: This is the scenario Renata described when asked to walk through what she would want Athelon to do on a typical major structural repair. It is not hypothetical — it is a composite of jobs she has run in the last three years, collapsed into a single sequence to illustrate the documentation chain.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > A Representative Major Repair — What the Workflow Should Look Like
excerpt_text: **Scenario:** A King Air B200 arrives with impact damage to the aft fuselage skin — a panel that was struck by ground support equipment. The damage is approximately 4.5 inches in the long axis, with a small crack propagating from the lower edge of the dent toward the aft pressure bulkhead frame. The crack changes the classification: this is not a dent-within-limits situation.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > A Representative Major Repair — What the Workflow Should Look Like
excerpt_text: **Work order opened:** She creates the work order. The system should prompt: *Is this a minor or major repair?* Not as a dropdown buried in "additional fields." As a classification step, up front, before task creation. She selects major repair. At that moment, the work order should display a banner or status block: **"Major Repair — Form 337 Required. 337 Reference: PENDING."** The banner should not be dismissible.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in product capability maturity (current state), suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > A Representative Major Repair — What the Workflow Should Look Like
excerpt_text: **SRM reference recorded:** She locates the applicable King Air B200 SRM section — SRM 51-10-00, Allowable Damage and Repair — and determines the damage exceeds limits. She records the full SRM reference: manual title, revision level, chapter, section, subject, and the specific table that establishes the limits. Athelon needs a structured reference field for this — manual, revision, CSS minimum — not a single text block. She records it.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in product capability maturity (current state) and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > A Representative Major Repair — What the Workflow Should Look Like
excerpt_text: **DER engaged:** The crack proximity to the pressure bulkhead frame means SRM repair coverage is insufficient — primary structure involvement requires a DER disposition. She contacts Alejandro Reyes. He reviews the photos and dimensional data she sends, issues a repair design drawing number and an approval memo. She gets the DER document number: *AR-2024-B200-0047.* She enters this in the DER reference field on the work order. If Athelon doesn't have a dedicated DER field, she writes it in the work scope description with a header. She prefers a dedicated field.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > A Representative Major Repair — What the Workflow Should Look Like
excerpt_text: **337 prepared:** The 337 is prepared before the repair starts. Box 1 (aircraft registration), Box 2 (make/model/serial), Box 3 (certificate number), Box 8 (description of work accomplished — this is where the repair gets fully described: damage found, dimensions, location by station reference, DER-approved repair method, materials and fasteners used, final condition). She assigns Gulf Coast's internal tracking number: *GCS-337-2024-0112.* She enters this in the `form_337_ref` field in Athelon. The work order banner updates: **"Major Repair — Form 337 Reference: GCS-337-2024-0112 (PENDING FAA SUBMISSION)."**
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in compliance defensibility and audit posture, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > A Representative Major Repair — What the Workflow Should Look Like
excerpt_text: **Repair performed:** Doubler fabricated per DER drawing. Rivet pattern installed per design. Corrosion treatment applied. Panel closed. Final inspection. Everything that matters about the repair — materials, fastener specifications, edge distances, final condition — goes in the maintenance record. Not because the FAA is coming. Because the aircraft is going to be maintained by someone else in five years.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > A Representative Major Repair — What the Workflow Should Look Like
excerpt_text: **337 submitted, FAA number returned:** The 337 is submitted electronically to Oklahoma City. The FAA returns a document number. She updates the `form_337_ref` field in Athelon from the internal tracking number to the FAA-issued number. The audit trail — old value, new value, date of update, who updated it — should be retained. The status updates: **"Major Repair — Form 337 Reference: [FAA number] (SUBMITTED)."**
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > A Representative Major Repair — What the Workflow Should Look Like
excerpt_text: **RTS authorization:** She opens the RTS authorization screen. It displays the full chain: N-number, serial, WO#, work description, repair classification (Major Repair), Form 337 reference and submission status, DER reference, her name and IA certificate number. She reviews it. She signs. The RTS is authorized and the maintenance record is locked.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for product capability maturity (current state) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Renata Vasquez — Sheet Metal / Structural Technician > A Representative Major Repair — What the Workflow Should Look Like

speaker: Rachel
excerpt_text: Manually. I subscribe to manufacturer revision notifications. section: Interview: Rachel Kwon — Tech Publications & Training Manager
excerpt_text: I wish the software would require a structured reference before a sign-off is accepted. Not free text. A structured field — document type, revision, chapter, section. Required to submit. If you don't fill it in, the step does not sign off. Full stop.

section: Interview: Rachel Kwon — Tech Publications & Training Manager
excerpt_text: The problem is that Corridor has no idea what any manual reference *means*. It's just text in a field. There's nothing that says "this task card refers to AMM Chapter 32-10, Rev 9, and Chapter 32-10 was revised to Rev 10 last week." The connection doesn't exist.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Interview: Rachel Kwon — Tech Publications & Training Manager
excerpt_text: What I want is a publications registry in the system — not the full text of the manuals, that's what Jeppesen is for — but a record of document type, document identifier, current revision, and effective date. When a mechanic references "AMM Rev 9, Ch 32-10," the system checks against the registry. If Rev 9 is not current, the system flags the reference. That would catch stale references before they become a discrepancy.

section: Profile: Rachel Kwon — Tech Publications & Training Manager > Background
excerpt_text: Rachel Kwon holds an A&P certificate with IA authorization and worked as a line mechanic and inspector for nine years before moving into the Tech Publications and Training role at Summit Aerospace Services in 2021. She took the job because the position was a new one — the shop's previous approach to approved data management was "we have a shared drive with some PDFs on it" — and she was frustrated enough with that approach to want to fix it.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Profile: Rachel Kwon — Tech Publications & Training Manager > Background
excerpt_text: Summit has 18 mechanics, 3 inspectors, and a DOM who knows how to run a shop but admits he doesn't have time to manage the publications library the way it needs to be managed. Rachel was hired to fix that. She built the current system — a combination of a Jeppesen Maintenance Data subscription, a shared network drive with strict folder conventions, and a paper-based training records binder — largely from scratch.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Profile: Rachel Kwon — Tech Publications & Training Manager > Background
excerpt_text: She has a strong opinion on one subject: when a mechanic does work, they should be able to point to exactly what approved data they used, down to the section and revision number. Not just "I used the AMM." Which AMM? Which revision? Which section? That is what an FAA inspector asks. That is what a defensible maintenance record contains. And that is what most MRO software, in her experience, makes it almost impossible to document properly.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for risk landscape and failure modes and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

The work had been done correctly (as it turned out), but the documentation reference was wrong. She considers that a near-miss.

section: Profile: Rachel Kwon — Tech Publications & Training Manager > Core Pain Points (Pre-Interview Summary)
excerpt_text: 1. **Task cards don't require specific approved data references.** Corridor has a notes field. Mechanics write "per AMM." Rachel wants section number, revision number, page number. Corridor has no enforcement mechanism for this.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Profile: Rachel Kwon — Tech Publications & Training Manager > Core Pain Points (Pre-Interview Summary)
excerpt_text: 2. **No link between the task card and the actual document.** "Per AMM Chapter 5-20-00, Rev 12" is just text in a field — it doesn't connect to the actual manual. If the manual gets revised and the reference becomes stale, there's nothing to flag it.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in risk landscape and failure modes that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Profile: Rachel Kwon — Tech Publications & Training Manager > Core Pain Points (Pre-Interview Summary)
excerpt_text: 3. **AD tracking lives in Excel.** She wants AD compliance documentation to be in the same system as work orders and task cards. The current separation means she has to manually cross-reference.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in compliance defensibility and audit posture, indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Profile: Rachel Kwon — Tech Publications & Training Manager > Core Pain Points (Pre-Interview Summary)
excerpt_text: 4. **New hires don't know how to document approved data references.** It's the most common training issue. Mechanics with prior experience from other shops have habits — bad habits, usually — around documentation. Retraining them is manual.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in risk landscape and failure modes that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Profile: Rachel Kwon — Tech Publications & Training Manager > Core Pain Points (Pre-Interview Summary)
excerpt_text: 5. **RSM changes have no distribution confirmation workflow.** When she revises the RSM, she emails the PDF to everyone and asks them to reply confirming receipt. About 60% reply. The other 40% she has to chase.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Profile: Rachel Kwon — Tech Publications & Training Manager > What She Wants from Software
excerpt_text: *"I want task cards to have a structured approved data reference field — not a free-text notes field. Part number, document type (AMM/SRM/IPC/etc.), chapter-section-subject, revision number. All required. If you don't fill it in, you can't sign off the step."*
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in risk landscape and failure modes and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Profile: Rachel Kwon — Tech Publications & Training Manager > What She Wants from Software
excerpt_text: *"I want the system to know which revision of which manual is current, and flag when a task card references a revision that's been superseded."*
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in risk landscape and failure modes that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Profile: Rachel Kwon — Tech Publications & Training Manager > What She Wants from Software
excerpt_text: *"ADs should live in the same system as work orders. When I open a work order for a Cirrus SR22, the system should show me every applicable AD and whether it's been addressed. Not a separate spreadsheet. The same system."*
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in risk landscape and failure modes, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Profile: Rachel Kwon — Tech Publications & Training Manager > What She Wants from Software
excerpt_text: *"When I update the RSM and publish a new revision, I want confirmation from every mechanic in the system — a required read-and-acknowledge that they can't dismiss without reading the affected sections."*

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: You've got a lot going on right now — thanks for making time. Can you describe what your morning actually looks like? Before the first customer call comes in.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Danny
excerpt_text: I get in around 7:30 and the first thing I do is look at my whiteboard. *(gestures at the personal desk whiteboard)* I know, it's low-tech. But it's actually accurate. I update it myself every day after I walk through the shop. Tail numbers, what's in there, roughly what stage they're at.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: You've got three systems: the whiteboard, Corridor, and the Word doc. Why three?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Danny
excerpt_text: Because none of them does what I need by itself. The whiteboard is the schedule — it's the DOM's and mine, it reflects reality because we update it ourselves. Corridor is the official record — work orders, task cards, all of that. But the Corridor list view just shows me work order numbers and status. I can't see what the current task is, I can't see parts status, I can't see notes without clicking into each job. The Word doc is my customer-facing brain. It has the information customers actually ask about in the format I can use to answer a call in 30 seconds.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: How many of those calls are "is my plane done yet?"
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for future-state roadmap and expansion vectors and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Danny
excerpt_text: *(exhales)* At least ten a day in busy season. On a Tuesday in May, half my calls are status calls. And here's the thing — I understand it. These are people's planes. Some of them depend on the plane for their business. Some of them just love their aircraft and they dropped it off and they feel out of control. I get it. But I can't do anything meaningful with their plane while I'm on the phone telling them it's "in progress."
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in future-state roadmap and expansion vectors and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: What would you give to not have those calls?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Danny
excerpt_text: I would give a lot. If every customer could log into a page and see — the aircraft is currently in bay 2, these are the tasks on the work order, these four are completed, this one is in progress right now, the next step is X — they would not call me. They'd refresh the page at midnight if they wanted to. I would still get calls, but they'd be meaningful calls. "I saw the prop came off, what did you find?" That's a call I can work with.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for future-state roadmap and expansion vectors and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Finn

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: Tell me about the difference between what a customer should see and what the mechanic sees.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in market/backstory and mission origin, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Danny
excerpt_text: Totally different things. The mechanic sees task steps, sign-offs, part numbers, torque values, references to the AMM. None of that means anything to an aircraft owner who's a dentist. The customer needs to see: is the aircraft being worked on right now? What did you find? What are you doing about it? What parts are you waiting for and when do you expect them? What's the estimated completion date?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for operational/governance discipline and controls and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: You mentioned authorization when discrepancies are found. Walk me through how that works now.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Danny
excerpt_text: Mechanic finds something during an inspection. He puts a note in Corridor and tells me. I call the customer. Customer says yes or no on the phone. If it's yes, I go into the work order notes field and type "Customer verbally authorized additional work per phone call [date] [time]." Then I send them an email summary and ask them to reply with written authorization. Fifty percent of the time I get the reply back within an hour. Fifty percent of the time I'm chasing it for a day or two.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: Let's talk about AOG. How do you handle a situation where something on an aircraft turns out to be much more urgent?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Danny
excerpt_text: AOG is different from everything else. When a work order goes AOG — aircraft on ground, can't fly — everything stops being normal. That plane is priority one. Parts get expedited regardless of cost. Mechanics shift off other jobs. I'm calling the customer every few hours whether they ask or not. It's a completely different operating mode.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in future-state roadmap and expansion vectors and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Finn
excerpt_text: *[AOG as a cross-cutting system state affecting queue order, notifications, visible elapsed timer across all roles.]*
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for field workflow realities (mobile/offline/hangar constraints) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: When you hand a work order off to billing and it comes back with issues, what kinds of things are wrong?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Danny
excerpt_text: Usually one of three things. Task card wasn't completed — a mechanic left a step unsigned and nobody caught it until I reviewed the work order on the way out. Parts on the job don't match the task card — something was swapped out during the job and the parts record wasn't updated. Or there's a discrepancy that was found and documented but customer authorization is missing.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in operational/governance discipline and controls that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Nadia
excerpt_text: One more question. If you could only fix one thing about how software handles your job today, what would it be?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
speaker: Danny
excerpt_text: Real-time status. I need to see what's happening on the floor without calling someone or physically walking out there. When a mechanic signs off a task step, I want to see that. When a part gets issued, I want to see that. When the aircraft moves from the shop to the run-up pad, I want to see that.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in product capability maturity (current state), suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: I wish the software would let me generate a read-only customer link for every work order. They see a customer view — translated language, no technical jargon, just status and what's pending. They don't see the internal notes. They don't see the dollar amounts in real time if I don't want them to. But they can see their plane is being worked on.

excerpt_text: **Danny:** Totally different things. The mechanic sees task steps, sign-offs, part numbers, torque values, references to the AMM. section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: And the status language matters. In Corridor, the status options are "Open," "In Progress," and "Closed." "In Progress" covers everything from "we haven't touched it yet but the work order is open" to "the mechanic is putting the cowling back on right now." Those are not the same thing. A customer who calls on day three of "In Progress" doesn't know if we've done anything.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: I want customer-facing status that means something. Things like: "Awaiting your aircraft arrival." "Aircraft received — initial inspection pending." "Inspection in progress." "Discrepancy found — authorization required." "Awaiting parts — estimated [date]." "Work complete — final inspection pending." "Ready for pickup." Seven clear stages the customer can understand without calling me.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for compliance defensibility and audit posture and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: **Danny:** Mechanic finds something during an inspection. He puts a note in Corridor and tells me. I call the customer. Customer says yes or no on the phone. If it's yes, I go into the work order notes field and type "Customer verbally authorized additional work per phone call [date] [time]." Then I send them an email summary and ask them to reply with written authorization. Fifty percent of the time I get the reply back within an hour. Fifty percent of the time I'm chasing it for a day or two.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: Meanwhile the mechanic can't close out that discrepancy until the authorization is documented. Or he can, in Corridor — there's nothing technically stopping him. I've had mechanics close out work orders before written authorization came in because they wanted to move on to the next job.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in future-state roadmap and expansion vectors and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: I wish the software would create a formal authorization workflow. Mechanic writes up a discrepancy. The system holds the work order in an "authorization pending" state. It generates an email to the customer with a link they can click to approve or decline. When they click, it timestamps the authorization, records their identity, and moves the work order forward. No chasing. No "I forgot to send the email." The system does it.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: **Nadia:** Let's talk about AOG. How do you handle a situation where something on an aircraft turns out to be much more urgent?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: **Danny:** AOG is different from everything else. When a work order goes AOG — aircraft on ground, can't fly — everything stops being normal. That plane is priority one. Parts get expedited regardless of cost. Mechanics shift off other jobs. I'm calling the customer every few hours whether they ask or not. It's a completely different operating mode.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in future-state roadmap and expansion vectors and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: In Corridor, AOG is a checkbox I can tick in the work order. It doesn't actually *do* anything. It doesn't reprioritize the parts queue. It doesn't move the work order to the top of everyone's list. It doesn't change what the DOM sees on her screen. It's just a tag that says "someone flagged this."
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for field workflow realities (mobile/offline/hangar constraints) and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: I wish the software would treat AOG as a system state, not just a label. When a work order goes AOG, it should be visible everywhere: at the top of every technician's work queue, at the top of the parts manager's list, at the top of the DOM's dashboard. There should be a timestamp on when it went AOG and a running elapsed time visible to everyone in the shop. Nothing makes a Part 145 shop look more professional to a customer than an AOG being handled with visible urgency. And nothing makes it look worse than "yeah, we flagged it as AOG but things are pretty busy."
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in compliance defensibility and audit posture, suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: **Nadia:** When you hand a work order off to billing and it comes back with issues, what kinds of things are wrong?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: **Danny:** Usually one of three things. Task card wasn't completed — a mechanic left a step unsigned and nobody caught it until I reviewed the work order on the way out. Parts on the job don't match the task card — something was swapped out during the job and the parts record wasn't updated. Or there's a discrepancy that was found and documented but customer authorization is missing.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in operational/governance discipline and controls that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: I do a manual review before every work order hits the billing queue. I look at every task card, I look at the parts list, I look at the authorization records. It takes me 15–20 minutes per work order depending on how complex it is.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in field workflow realities (mobile/offline/hangar constraints) that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: I wish the software would do that review automatically. Show me a pre-close checklist when I try to move a work order to billing. "Task cards: 7 complete, 0 unsigned." "Parts: all installed parts have documentation on file." "Discrepancies: 3 found, 3 authorized." Green means ready to close. Red means here's what's missing, go fix it.
interpretation: This excerpt highlights a recurring friction point in operational/governance discipline and controls that can propagate into compliance or execution risk.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: **Nadia:** One more question. If you could only fix one thing about how software handles your job today, what would it be?
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: **Danny:** Real-time status. I need to see what's happening on the floor without calling someone or physically walking out there. When a mechanic signs off a task step, I want to see that. When a part gets issued, I want to see that. When the aircraft moves from the shop to the run-up pad, I want to see that.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in product capability maturity (current state), suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: Not because I'm micromanaging. Because customers call me. And I can either be the person who knows what's happening and can tell them something real, or I can be the person who says "let me check and call you back" seventeen times a day. I want to be the first one. Right now, Corridor makes me the second one.
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for strategic differentiation vs legacy incumbents and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Interview: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service
excerpt_text: *[Session ends. Danny shows Nadia and Finn his Word document status sheet — it's 47 rows long and covers every aircraft currently in work or awaiting scheduling. Finn photographs it with Danny's permission. It becomes the primary design reference for the Athelon customer-facing status feature.]*
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Profile: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service > Core Pain Points (Pre-Interview Summary)
excerpt_text: 1. **No real-time status from the shop floor.** He's always a step behind. The customer knows the aircraft was dropped off 3 days ago; he doesn't know whether it's been touched yet.

excerpt_text: 2. **Customer-facing status is meaningless.** "In Progress" doesn't tell anyone anything. He needs granular, translatable status that he can communicate to a customer without making them panic.

section: Profile: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service > Core Pain Points (Pre-Interview Summary)
excerpt_text: 3. **AOG has no dedicated workflow.** When a work order goes AOG, everything should reorganize around it. In Corridor, he has to manually communicate the priority change to every party. There's no system-level flag that tells the shop "this one first, everything else waits."
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in product capability maturity (current state), suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Profile: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service > Core Pain Points (Pre-Interview Summary)
excerpt_text: 4. **Verbal authorization tracking is done on sticky notes.** When a customer verbally authorizes additional work, Danny writes it down, puts it in the work order notes field in Corridor, and then has to chase the customer for a follow-up email or signature. There's no workflow for authorization — it's a free-text field and hope.
interpretation: This excerpt evidences capability momentum in product capability maturity (current state), indicating operational fit is improving.

section: Profile: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service > Core Pain Points (Pre-Interview Summary)
excerpt_text: 5. **He can't show customers anything.** Customers ask "can I log in and see the status?" The answer is no. He wishes he could send them a link.
interpretation: Explicit requirement language identifies a high-consequence gap in risk landscape and failure modes and implies mandatory control hardening.

section: Profile: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service > What He Wants from Software
excerpt_text: *"I want a customer portal so bad. Just a read-only link I can send to an owner. They can see the status, the task list, what's done, what's pending. They stop calling me for status and I can actually do my job."*
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for operational/governance discipline and controls and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Profile: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service > What He Wants from Software
excerpt_text: *"AOG should change the color of the work order and put it at the top of every list, everywhere in the shop, until it's done. It shouldn't be a status I have to explain to people."*
interpretation: Explicit requirement language defines a concrete build target in product capability maturity (current state), suitable for implementation tracking.

section: Profile: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service > What He Wants from Software
excerpt_text: *"When a mechanic signs off a task step, I want to see it. I don't want to refresh. I want my screen to update and I want to be able to tell the customer 'they just finished the engine runup' without it being a lie."*
interpretation: This excerpt provides contextual signal for market/backstory and mission origin and supports synthesis when paired with corroborating records.

section: Profile: Danny Osei — Work Order Coordinator / Customer Service > What He Wants from Software
excerpt_text: *"The customer-facing status and the internal status should be different fields. What the mechanic sees is technical. What the customer sees should be translated — something like 'Inspection in progress' or 'Awaiting parts — estimated 2 days.'"*
